Name: BROWNFIELDS PROJECTS
Title: 79 - BROWNFIELDS PROJECTS
Laws: COMPREHENSIVE ENVIRONMENTAL RESPONSE, COMPENSATION, AND LIABILITY ACT, AS AMENDED BY THE SMALL BUSINESS LIABILITY RELIEF AND BROWNFIELDS REVITALIZATION ACT, 42 UNITED STATES CODE 9601 ET SEQ. ? SECTIONS 101, 104 (K), AND 107.
Narrative: EPA AWARDS GRANTS AND COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS AUTHORIZED BY CERCLA SECTION ?104(K) THROUGH A COMPETITIVE PROCESS WITH A RANKING SYSTEM THAT INCLUDES FACTORS RELATING TO COMMUNITY NEED, IMPACT ON HUMAN HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT, STIMULATION OR LEVERAGING OF OTHER FUNDS, ELIGIBILITY FOR FUNDING FROM OTHER SOURCES, AND EFFECTIVE USE OF EXISTING INFRASTRUCTURE.  THIS STATEMENT APPLIES TO ALL 104(K) GRANTS.  IN ADDITION, THE AGENCY HAS AUTHORITY UNDER CERCLA SECTION ? 104(K) TO PERFORM BROWNFIELDS SITE ASSESSMENTS ITSELF AND DIRECTLY PROVIDE RESEARCH, TRAINING, AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE SERVICES TO BROWNFIELDS COMMUNITIES.  EACH FISCAL YEAR, THE BROWNFIELDS PROGRAM MUST ALLOCATE 25% OF THE TOTAL AVAILABLE FUNDS FOR CERCLA SECTION 104(K) GRANTS TO ADDRESS SITES CONTAMINATED BY PETROLEUM.REGIONS:BROWNFIELDS COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS FOR ASSESSMENT, REVOLVING LOAN FUND AND CLEANUP - CERCLA SECTION ? 104(K)(2)&(3)ASSESSMENT COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS:  ASSESSMENT COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS PROVIDE FUNDING TO INVENTORY, CHARACTERIZE, ASSESS, AND CONDUCT PLANNING AND COMMUNITY INVOLVEMENT RELATED TO BROWNFIELDS SITES. THE AGENCY FUNDS BROWNFIELDS ASSESSMENT COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS FOR UP TO $200,000 EACH.  APPLICANTS MAY REQUEST A WAIVER OF THE $200,000 LIMIT UP TO $350,000 FOR A SPECIFIC SITE.  WAIVERS ARE BASED ON THE ANTICIPATED LEVEL OF CONTAMINATION, SIZE, OR OWNERSHIP STATUS OF THE SITE.REVOLVING LOAN FUND COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS:  REVOLVING LOAN FUND COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS PROVIDE FUNDING FOR A RECIPIENT TO CAPITALIZE ON A REVOLVING LOAN FUND AND PROVIDE SUB-GRANTS TO CARRY OUT CLEANUP ACTIVITIES AT BROWNFIELDS SITES. THE AGENCY FUNDS BROWNFIELDS REVOLVING LOAN FUND COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS FOR UP TO $1,000,000 PER ELIGIBLE ENTITY, INCLUDING COALITIONS OF ELIGIBLE ENTITIES.  IN ADDITION, THE BROWNFIELDS LAW (CERCLA 104(K)(3)(D)) BCRLF PILOTS MAY TRANSITION INTO CERCLA 104(K)(3) GRANT PROGRAM USING PRE-FY 2003 SUPERFUND APPROPRIATION.CLEANUP COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS:  CLEANUP COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS PROVIDE DIRECT FUNDING TO CARRY OUT CLEANUP ACTIVITIES AT BROWNFIELDS SITES. THE AGENCY FUNDS BROWNFIELDS CLEANUP COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS FOR UP TO $200,000 PER SITE FOR NO MORE THAN FIVE SITES TO ELIGIBLE ENTITIES AND NON PROFIT ORGANIZATIONS TO CLEANUP BROWNFIELDS SITES THEY OWN.  HEADQUARTERS AND REGIONS:BROWNFIELDS K6 GRANTS  - CERCLA SECTION 104(K)(6)TRAINING, RESEARCH AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE GRANTS:  FUNDING OF BROWNFIELDS TRAINING, RESEARCH, AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE GRANTS, COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS, CONTRACTS, AND INTERAGENCY AGREEMENTS IS AUTHORIZED UNDER CERCLA SECTION 104(K)(6), AS  AMENDED, 42 U.S.C. 9604(K)(6). THIS STATUTE AUTHORIZES EPA TO PROVIDE, OR FUND ELIGIBLE ENTITIES OR NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS TO PROVIDE, BROWNFIELDS TRAINING, RESEARCH, AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO INDIVIDUALS AND ORGANIZATIONS.  ELIGIBLE GRANT APPLICANTS ARE LIMITED TO GOVERNMENTAL APPLICANTS AND NON-PROFIT ORGANIZATIONS.  PUBLIC AND PRIVATE UNIVERSITIES ARE ELIGIBLE AS WELL.  FUNDING FOR FINANCIAL ASSISTANCE, CONTRACTS, AND INTERAGENCY AGREEMENTS AWARDED UNDER CERCLA SECTION ?104(K)(6) IS LIMITED, BY STATUTE TO NO MORE THAN 15% OF THE TOTAL AMOUNT AVAILABLE FOR CERCLA SECTION 104(K) GRANTS.   (HQ, RT)BROWNFIELDS AREA-WIDE PLANNING GRANT PROGRAM:  UNDER CERCLA 104(K)(6) AUTHORITY, EPA AWARDS COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS AT UP TO $200,000 OVER A TWO YEAR PERIOD TO HELP ELIGIBLE ENTITIES FACILITATE COMMUNITY INVOLVEMENT AND CONDUCT RESEARCH, TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO DEVELOP BROWNFIELDS AREA-WIDE PLANS AND PLAN IMPLEMENTATION STRATEGIES.   THE BROWNFIELDS AREA-WIDE PLANNING GRANT FUNDING RESULTS IN AN AREA-WIDE PLAN (INCLUDING IMPLEMENTATION STRATEGIES) FOR AN AREA AFFECTED BY ONE OR MORE BROWNFIELD SITES.  THE BROWNFIELDS AREA-WIDE PLAN WILL INFORM THE ASSESSMENT, CLEANUP AND REUSE OF BROWNFIELDS PROPERTIES AND PROMOTE AREA-WIDE REVITALIZATION.  ELIGIBLE GRANT APPLICANTS ARE LIMITED TO GOVERNMENTAL APPLICANTS AND NON-PROFIT ORGANIZATIONS.  PUBLIC AND PRIVATE UNIVERSITIES ARE ELIGIBLE AS WELL   (HQ, RT).TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO BROWNFIELDS COMMUNITIES PROGRAM:  THE TAB GRANTS ARE AUTHORIZED UNDER CERCLA 104(K)(6) AND PROVIDE TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO COMMUNITIES ON  BROWNFIELDS ASSESSMENT, CLEANUP, AND REUSE RELATED-ISSUES.  THE TAB GRANTS ARE AWARDED TO ELIGIBLE ENTITIES TO PROVIDE TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO COMMUNITIES AND OTHER STAKEHOLDERS HELPING THEM TACKLE THE CHALLENGE OF ASSESSING, CLEANING UP, AND PREPARING BROWNFIELDS SITES FOR REDEVELOPMENT, ESPECIALLY UNDERSERVED, RURAL, SMALL, AND OTHERWISE DISTRESSED COMMUNITIES. THE TAB GRANTS VARY IN SIZE FROM $500,000 TO $1,000,000 DEPENDING ON THE GEOGRAPHICAL AREA COVERED.  THE TAB-PROJECT IMPLEMENTATION GRANTS ARE A VARIATION OF THE TAB GRANT AND ARE FOCUSED ON PROVIDING TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO COMMUNITIES AND OTHER STAKEHOLDERS ON IMPLEMENTING A SPECIFIC PROJECT(S).  THE TAB-PI GRANTS ARE ANTICIPATED TO RANGE IN SIZE FROM $200,000-$500,000. THE PROJECT PERIOD FOR BOTH TAB AND TAB-PI GRANTS IS FIVE YEARS. (HQ, RT).JOB TRAINING GRANT PROGRAM:  CERCLA 104(K)(6) PROVIDES THE AUTHORITY FOR THE BROWNFIELDS JOB TRAINING GRANTS PROGRAM, A SPECIAL SUB-SET OF THE CERCLA SECTION 104(K)(6) COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS.  UNDER THE JOB TRAINING GRANTS PROGRAM, EPA AWARDS COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS AT UP TO $200,000 OVER A TWO YEAR PERIOD TO HELP LOCAL CITIZENS TAKE ADVANTAGE OF NEW JOBS CREATED BY ASSESSMENT AND CLEANUP OF BROWNFIELDS.  APPLICANTS MUST BE EITHER ELIGIBLE GOVERNMENTAL ENTITIES OR ELIGIBLE NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS AND THE GRANTS WILL BE LIMITED TO AREAS WITHIN OR NEAR A BROWNFIELDS GRANT COMMUNITY. EPA ALSO PROVIDES FUNDING TO THE NATIONAL INSTITUTE FOR ENVIRONMENTAL HEALTH SCIENCES (NIEHS) INTERAGENCY AGREEMENT TO SUPPORT WORKER TRAINING FOR BROWNFIELDS SITES.   (HQ, RT).EPA TARGETED BROWNFIELDS ASSESSMENTS (TBAS):  EPA USES STAG FUNDS TO PERFORM LABORATORY ANALYSES AT TARGETED BROWNFIELDS SITES WHEN REQUESTED TO DO SO BY NON-FEDERAL PARTIES ELIGIBLE FOR GRANT FUNDING UNDER SECTION CERCLA 104(K) OR CERCLA 128.  LABORATORY ANALYSES THAT THE AGENCY ITSELF REQUIRES TO ADMINISTER THE BROWNFIELDS PROGRAM MUST BE FUNDED WITH APPROPRIATIONS IN THE EPM ACCOUNT.  (HQ, RT).BROWNFIELDS NATIONAL PARTNERSHIP:  EPA IS REQUIRED TO COORDINATE WITH OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES IN CERCLA SECTIONS 104(K)(4) AND 104(K)(5).  TO COMPLY WITH THIS REQUIREMENT, THE EPA AND ITS PARTNERING FEDERAL AGENCIES CONFIRMED THEIR COMMITMENT TO WORK TOGETHER BY DEVELOPING THE BROWNFIELDS FEDERAL PARTNERSHIP ACTION AGENDA IN NOVEMBER 2002. THE BROWNFIELDS FEDERAL PARTNERSHIP ACTION AGENDA REPRESENTS THE FOCUSED EFFORTS OF OVER 20 FEDERAL AGENCIES, MAKING OVER 100 COMMITMENTS TO WORK TOGETHER IN A TIMELY MANNER TO HELP COMMUNITIES MORE EFFECTIVELY PREVENT, ASSESS, SAFELY CLEAN UP, AND SUSTAINABLE REUSE BROWNFIELDS.  (HQ, RT)PRE-FY 2003 SUPERFUND CARRYOVERCOMPLETION OF BROWNFIELDS PILOT GRANTS, EPA EXPENDS PRE-FY 2003 SUPERFUND APPROPRIATIONS FOR PREVIOUSLY AWARDED BROWNFIELDS PILOT GRANTS AWARDED UNDER CERCLA SECTION ?104(D).  THESE GRANTS WILL TERMINATE FOLLOWING COMPLETION OF THE SCOPE OF WORK.  (HQ, RT)
Definition: BROWNFIELDS ARE DEFINED AS REAL PROPERTIES, THE EXPANSION, REDEVELOPMENT, OR REUSE, WHICH MAY BE COMPLICATED BY THE PRESENCE OR POTENTIAL PRESENCE OF A HAZARDOUS SUBSTANCE, POLLUTANT, OR CONTAMINANT.  (THERE IS SOME STATUTORY EXCLUSION TO THIS DEFINITION).THE BROWNFIELDS PROGRAM IS DESIGNED TO EMPOWER STATES, TRIBES, LOCAL COMMUNITIES, AND OTHER STAKEHOLDERS IN ECONOMIC REDEVELOPMENT TO WORK TOGETHER TO PREVENT, ASSESS, SAFELY CLEANUP, AND REUSE BROWNFIELDS

Name: INFRASTRUCTURE ASSISTANCE:  CLEAN WATER SRF
Title: 80 - INFRASTRUCTURE ASSISTANCE:  CLEAN WATER SRF
Laws: CLEAN WATER ACT, TITLE VI
Narrative: THE CLEAN WATER STATE REVOLVING FUND PROGRAM PROVIDES FEDERAL FUNDS TO THE STATES AND PUERTO RICO TO CAPITALIZE REVOLVING LOAN FUND PROGRAMS. FUNDS ARE THEN PROVIDED FOR THE HIGHEST PRIORITY PROJECTS, INCLUDING THOSE INVOLVING TRADITIONAL WASTEWATER, ABATEMENT OF STORM-WATER AND COMBINED SEWER OVERFLOWS, NON-POINT SOURCE CONTROL, ESTUARY MANAGEMENT, AND OTHERS. IN AN EFFORT TO BETTER ADDRESS THE SERIOUS HEALTH PROBLEMS THAT FACE INDIAN TRIBES, THE PROGRAM ALSO INCLUDES A PROVISION FOR INDIAN SET-ASIDE GRANT FUNDS.ALL REMAINING EXPENDITURES FROM THE CONSTRUCTION GRANTS PROGRAM (TITLE II OF THE CLEAN WATER ACT), WHICH PRECEDED THE CLEAN WATER SRF, SHOULD BE CHARGED TO THIS PROGRAM/PROJECT.
Definition: PROVIDES FEDERAL FUNDS TO THE STATES AND PUERTO RICO TO CAPITALIZE REVOLVING LOAN FUND PROGRAMS.

Name: INFRASTRUCTURE ASSISTANCE:  DRINKING WATER SRF
Title: 81 - INFRASTRUCTURE ASSISTANCE:  DRINKING WATER SRF
Laws: SAFE DRINKING WATER ACT (SDWA), SECTION 1452
Narrative: THE DWSRF IS DESIGNED TO HELP PUBLIC WATER SYSTEMS FINANCE THE COSTS OF INFRASTRUCTURE IMPROVEMENTS NEEDED TO ACHIEVE OR MAINTAIN COMPLIANCE WITH SDWA REQUIREMENTS AND TO PROTECT PUBLIC HEALTH.  CAPITALIZATION GRANT FUNDS MAY ALSO BE USED BY STATES TO PROVIDE OTHER TYPES OF ASSISTANCE TO PROMOTE PREVENTION AND TO ENCOURAGE STRONGER DRINKING WATER SYSTEM MANAGEMENT PROGRAMS.  TO REDUCE OCCURRENCES OF SERIOUS PUBLIC HEALTH THREATS AND TO ENSURE SAFE DRINKING WATER SOURCES NATIONWIDE, THE EPA ADMINISTRATOR IS AUTHORIZED TO MAKE CAPITALIZATION GRANTS TO STATES, WHICH IN TURN CAN PROVIDE LOW-COST LOANS AND OTHER ASSISTANCE TO ELIGIBLE SYSTEMS.  RESOURCES MAY ALSO FUND INTER-AGENCY AGREEMENTS (IAS) TO OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES, SUCH AS THE INDIAN HEALTH SERVICE IN HHS, THAT PROVIDE SAFE DRINKING WATER ACTIVITIES IN SUPPORT OF THE TRIBES.
Definition: THIS PROGRAM IS DESIGNED TO HELP PUBLIC WATER SYSTEMS FINANCE THE COSTS OF INFRASTRUCTURE IMPROVEMENTS NEEDED TO ACHIEVE OR MAINTAIN COMPLIANCE WITH SDWA REQUIREMENTS AND TO PROTECT PUBLIC HEALTH.

Name: INFRASTRUCTURE ASSISTANCE:  MEXICO BORDER
Title: 82 - INFRASTRUCTURE ASSISTANCE:  MEXICO BORDER
Laws: CLEAN WATER ACT SECTION 510
Narrative: THE UNITED STATES AND MEXICO SHARE MORE THAN 2,000 MILES OF COMMON BORDER.. MORE THAN 14.6 MILLION PEOPLE LIVE IN THE BORDER AREA, MOSTLY IN FIFTEEN "SISTER CITY" PAIRS.  THE RAPID INCREASE IN POPULATION AND INDUSTRIALIZATION IN THE BORDER CITIES HAS OVERWHELMED EXISTING WASTEWATER TREATMENT AND DRINKING WATER SUPPLY FACILITIES.  UNTREATED SEWAGE OFTEN FLOWS NORTH INTO THE U.S. FROM TIJUANA, MEXICO AND NOGALES, AND INTO THE RIO GRANDE.  EPA WORKS CLOSELY WITH THE BORDER ENVIRONMENT COOPERATION COMMISSION (BECC) AND THE NORTH AMERICAN DEVELOPMENT BANK (NADB) TO EVALUATE ENVIRONMENTAL NEEDS AND TO FACILITATE THE CONSTRUCTION OF ENVIRONMENTAL INFRASTRUCTURE THROUGH THE PROVISION OF GRANT FUNDING FOR THE PLANNING, DESIGN, AND CONSTRUCTION OF HIGH PRIORITY WATER AND WASTEWATER TREATMENT INFRASTRUCTURE ON THE U.S./MEXICO BORDER.
Definition: ACTIVITIES IN CONJUNCTION WITH OTHER FEDERAL, STATE, TRIBAL AND LOCAL AGENCIES, TO IDENTIFY AND ADDRESS ENVIRONMENTAL PROBLEMS ALONG U.S. MEXICAN BORDER.

Name: HUMAN HEALTH RISK ASSESSMENT
Title: 84 - HUMAN HEALTH RISK ASSESSMENT
Laws: CAA AMENDMENTS, 42 U.S.C. 7403 ET SEQ. - SECTIONS 103, 108, 109, AND 112; CERCLA (SUPERFUND, 1980) SECTION 209(A) OF PUBLIC LAW 99-499; FIFRA (7 U.S.C. S/S 136 ET SEQ. (1996), AS AMENDED), SEC. 3(C)(2)(A); FQPA PL 104-170; SDWA (1996) 42 U.S.C. SECTION 300J-18; TSCA (PUBLIC LAW 94-469): 15 U.S.C. S/S 2601 ET SEQ. (1976), SEC. 4(B)(1)(B), SEC. 4(B)(2)(B).
Narrative: IN FY13, THE EPA RESEARCH WILL LOOK AT PROBLEMS FROM A SYSTEMS PERSPECTIVE.APPROACHING RESEARCH IN THIS WAY WILL BETTER ENABLE THE EPA TO DEVELOP SUSTAINABLE SOLUTIONS TO SOME OF OUR MOST PRESSING ENVIRONMENTAL PROBLEMS. HHRA WILL CONTRIBUTE TO THESE SOLUTIONS BY DEVELOPING SCIENTIFIC PRODUCTS AND LEVERAGING THE DIVERSE CAPABILITIES OF IN-HOUSE RESEARCH PLANS WILL INCORPORATE INPUT FROM EXTERNAL STAKEHOLDERS SUCH AS FEDERAL, STATE AND LOCAL GOVERNMENT AGENCIES, NON-GOVERNMENTAL ORGANIZATIONS, INDUSTRY, AND COMMUNITIES AFFECTED BY ENVIRONMENTAL PROBLEMS.UNLESS OTHERWISE NOTED, THE FOLLOWING HHRA ACTIVITIES PRIMARILY ARE FUNDED UNDER THE S&T APPROPRIATION.DEVELOP NUMEROUS IRIS AND OTHER HEALTH HAZARD ASSESSMENTS, PARTICULARLY FOR HIGH PRIORITY CHEMICALS (E.G. INORGANIC ARSENIC, CHROMIUM VI, METHANOL, CUMULATIVE PHTHALATE ASSESSMENT, LIBBY ASBESTOS (CANCER), AND PCB (NON-CANCER)).    DEVELOP APPROACHES FOR APPLYING MODE OF ACTION IN RISK ASSESSMENT AND IMPROVING QUANTIFICATION OF HEALTH RISKS (E.G. PBPK AND BBDR MODELING) AS WELL AS CHARACTERIZING ENVIRONMENTAL EXPOSURE AND RISK TO SUSCEPTIBLE POPULATIONS.DEVELOP INTEGRATED SCIENCE ASESSMENTS OF CRITERIA AIR POLLUTANTS (E.G. HEALTH EFFECTS OF NITROGEN OXIDES AND SULFUR OXIDES) AS A MANDATED PREREQUISITE TO EPA?S REVIEW OF THE NAAQS.  PROVIDE SCIENCE ASSESSMENT SUPPORT ON CRISIS-LEVEL CHEMICAL CONTAMINANT ISSUES (E.G. HURRICANE KATRINA, BP OIL SPILL).CONTINUE IMPROVING THE HEALTH AND ENVIRONMENT RESEARCH ONLINE (HERO) DATABASE TO SUPPORT A MORE CONTINUOUS PROCESS TO IDENTIFY, COMPILE, CHARACTERIZE, AND PRIORITIZE NEW SCIENTIFIC STUDIES FOR HUMAN HEALTH AND ECOLOGICAL ASSESSMENTS.EXPLORE MULTI-POLLUTANT ASSESSMENT APPROACHES AS CALLED FOR BY THE 2008, CLEAN AIR SCIENTIFIC ADVISORY COMMITTEE (CASAC) CONSULTATION ON EPA?S DRAFT PLAN FOR REVIEW OF THE PRIMARY NAAQS FOR CARBON MONOXIDE AND THE 2004, NAS REPORT ON AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT.DEVELOP PEER REVIEWED PROVISIONAL TOXICITY VALUES (PPRTVS) FOR EVALUATING CHEMICAL SPECIFIC EXPOSURES AT SUPERFUND SITES WHERE IRIS VALUES ARE UNAVAILABLE AND PROVIDE SUPPORT FOR THESE ASSESSMENTS THROUGH EPA'S SUPERFUND TECHNICAL SUPPORT CENTERS.DEVELOP EXPOSURE-RESPONSE DATA ARRAYS AND CUMULATIVE RISK TOOLS TO BETTER ESTIMATE POTENTIAL EFFECTS OF EXPOSURES AT SUPERFUND SITES ON HUMANS AND THE CONSULTATIVE SUPPORT NECESSARY FOR THE APPLICATION OF THESE METHODS.  THIS PROGRAM/PROJECT CAN USE FUNDS TO OBTAIN SEE PROGRAM RESOURCES.
Definition: THE EPA?S HUMAN HEALTH RISK ASSESSMENT (HHRA) RESEARCH PROGRAM GENERATES HEALTH ASSESSMENTS AND SCIENTIFIC PRODUCTS THAT ARE USED EXTENSIVELY BY THE EPA PROGRAM AND REGIONAL OFFICES, AND OTHER PARTIES TO ESTIMATE THE POTENTIAL RISK TO PUBLIC HEALTH FROM EXPOSURE TO ENVIRONMENTAL CONTAMINANTS. THESE ASSESSMENTS ENABLE THE AGENCY TO BETTER PREDICT AND PREVENT RISK AND PROVIDE A CRITICAL PART OF THE SCIENTIFIC FOUNDATION FOR THE DEVELOPMENT OF REGULATORY STANDARDS, GUIDELINES AND PRIORITIES AND THE THE MANAGEMENT OF ENVIRONMENTAL CLEANUPS.EXAMPLES OF HHRA PRODUCTS INCLUDE INDEPENDENTLY PEER REVIEWED HUMAN HEALTH ASSESSMENTS FOR INDIVIDUAL CHEMICALS AND CHEMICAL MIXTURES; INTEGRATED SCIENCE ASSESSMENTS FOR CRITERIA AIR POLLUTANTS/ RAPID RISK ASSESSMENTS AND TECHNICAL SUPPORT TO MEET PARTNER AND STAKEHOLDER NEEDS; AND METHODS AND MODELS TO MODERNIZE HUMAN HEALTH RISK ASSESSMENT.  THE PROGRAM IS INCREASINGLY FOCUSING ON STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT AND APPROACHING RESEARCH IN AN INTEGRATED, TRANSDISCIPLINARY FASHION TO DELIVER MORE INNOVATIVE, SUSTAINABLE SOLUTIONS TO OUR ENVIRONMENTAL PROBLEMS.

Name: LUST / UST
Title: 86 - LUST / UST
Laws: SOLID WASTE DISPOSAL ACT OF 1976, SECTION 8001(A) AND (B) AS AMENDED BY THE HAZARDOUS AND SOLID WASTE AMENDMENTS OF 1984 (P.L. 98-616); 42 U.S.C.  6991; RESOURCE CONSERVATION AND RECOVERY ACT OF 1976;  TRIBAL GRANTS (P.L. 105-276, 1998); ENVIRONMENTAL PROGRAMS ASSISTANCE ACT" PUBLIC LAW 98-313 (1984); AND EPA'S ANNUAL APPROPRIATIONS ACT.
Narrative: EPA?S LEAKING UNDERGROUND STORAGE TANK (LUST) PROGRAM PROMOTES RAPID AND EFFECTIVE RESPONSES TO RELEASES FROM FEDERALLY-REGULATED UNDERGROUND STORAGE TANKS (USTS) CONTAINING PETROLEUM AND HAZARDOUS SUBSTANCES BY ENHANCING STATE, LOCAL, AND TRIBAL ENFORCEMENT AND RESPONSE CAPABILITY. EPA WORKS WITH STATE AND TRIBAL UST PROGRAMS AS WELL AS NON-GOVERNMENTAL ORGANIZATIONS TO CLEAN UP LUST SITES, PROMOTE INNOVATIVE AND ENVIRONMENTALLY FRIENDLY APPROACHES IN CORRECTIVE ACTION IN ORDER TO ENHANCE AND STREAMLINE THE REMEDIATION PROCESS, AND MEASURE AND EVALUATE NATIONAL PROGRAM PROGRESS AND PERFORMANCE.  IN ADDITION, THE ENERGY POLICY ACT (EPACT) OF 2005 AUTHORIZED LUST TRUST FUND RESOURCES TO DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT A STRATEGY TO IMPLEMENT AND ENFORCE EPACT REQUIREMENTS CONCERNING USTS IN INDIAN COUNTRY.  EPA HAS PRIMARY RESPONSIBILITY FOR IMPLEMENTING THE LUST PROGRAM IN INDIAN COUNTRY, AND WILL USE A PORTION OF ITS LUST FUNDING FOR THESE ACTIVITIES, INCLUDING PROVIDING TRIBES WITH FINANCIAL ASSISTANCE FOR CLEANUPS. OUST WILL USE LUST FUNDING TO CONTINUE TO HELP THE REGIONS, STATES AND TRIBES IMPROVE LUST CLEANUP PERFORMANCE BY PERFORMING ANALYSES, SUCH AS THE CHARACTERIZATION OF THE BACKLOG OF STATES AND THE ASSESSMENT OF STATES? FINANCIAL SOUNDNESS, INCLUDING INSURANCE AND STATE CLEANUP FUNDS, A SIGNIFICANT SOURCE OF FUNDING FOR ADDRESSING LUST CLEANUPS.  IN ADDITION, THE PROGRAM WILL CONTINUE TO PROVIDE GUIDANCE AND TECHNICAL SUPPORT REGARDING CLEANUP APPROACHES AND TECHNOLOGIES, AND IMPLEMENT STRATEGIES TO REDUCE THE BACKLOG OF OPEN RELEASES.  EPA WILL CONTINUE TO WORK WITH STATES TO ADDRESS SITES STILL REQUIRING REMEDIATION.  THE AGENCY HAS PRIMARY RESPONSIBILITY FOR IMPLEMENTING THE LUST PROGRAM IN INDIAN COUNTRY.  THEREFORE, EPA USES A PORTION OF ITS LUST FUNDING TO IMPLEMENT THE LUST PROGRAM IN INDIAN COUNTRY (INCLUDING, BUT NOT LIMITED TO CLEANUP ACTIVITIES AND ENFORCEMENT).  THE EPACT REQUIREMENT TO DEVELOP A STRATEGY FOR IMPLEMENTING THE PROGRAM IN INDIAN COUNTRY ENHANCED EPA?S EFFORTS AND PROVIDED RENEWED FOCUS TO REDUCE THE CLEANUP BACKLOG AND TO PREVENT FUTURE RELEASES IN INDIAN COUNTRY.  TO ADDRESS LEAKING USTS IN INDIAN COUNTRY AND PROTECT VULNERABLE POPULATIONS, EPA WILL PROVIDE SUPPORT FOR SITE ASSESSMENTS, INVESTIGATIONS AND REMEDIATION; ENFORCEMENT AGAINST RESPONSIBLE PARTIES; CLEANUP OF SOIL AND/OR GROUNDWATER; ALTERNATE WATER SUPPLIES; AND COST RECOVERY AGAINST UST OWNERS AND OPERATORS.  EPA ALSO WILL PROVIDE TECHNICAL EXPERTISE AND ASSISTANCE BY UTILIZING IN-HOUSE PERSONNEL, CONTRACTORS AND GRANTS/COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS TO TRIBAL ENTITIES; RESPONSE ACTIVITIES; OVERSIGHT OF RESPONSIBLE PARTY LEAD CLEANUPS; AND SUPPORT AND ASSISTANCE TO TRIBAL GOVERNMENTS.  FUNDS PROVIDED UNDER THIS PROGRAM/PROJECT MAY BE UTILIZED TO OBTAIN SEE PROGRAM RESOURCES TO SUPPORT EPA HEADQUARTERS AND/OR REGIONAL OFFICES UNDER THE AUTHORITY OF THE "ENVIRONMENTAL PROGRAMS ASSISTANCE ACT" PUBLIC LAW 98-313, JUNE 12, 1984.
Definition: PROVIDES OVERSIGHT AND FINANCIAL ASSISTANCE FOR STATES, TRIBES, INTERTRIBAL CONSORTIA AND NON-GOVERNMENTAL ORGANIZATIONS.  ACTIVITIES INCLUDE PROVIDING TECHNICAL INFORMATION, FORUMS FOR INFORMATION EXCHANGE, AND TRAINING OPPORTUNITIES TO ENCOURAGE PROGRAM DEVELOPMENT AND/OR IMPLEMENTATION.

Name: LUST COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS
Title: 87 - LUST COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS
Laws: SOLID WASTE DISPOSAL ACT OF 1976 (SWDA), AS AMENDED, SECTION 9003(H), PUBLIC LAW 105-276 (1998), SUPPLEMENTAL APPROPRIATIONS FROM THE LEAKING UNDERGROUND STORAGE TANKS (LUST) TRUST FUND PROVIDED BY THE AMERICAN RECOVERY AND REINVESTMENT ACT OF 2009 (HEREINAFTER "RECOVERY ACT") AND EPA'S ANNUAL APPROPRIATION ACT.
Narrative: CHALLENGING AND COMPLEX ENVIRONMENTAL PROBLEMS, SUCH AS CONTAMINATED SOIL, SEDIMENT, AND GROUNDWATER THAT CAN CAUSE HUMAN HEALTH CONCERNS, PERSIST AT MANY CONTAMINATED PROPERTIES. THE LEAKING UNDERGROUND STORAGE TANK (LUST) PROGRAM PROMOTES RAPID AND EFFECTIVE RESPONSES TO RELEASES FROM UNDERGROUND STORAGE TANKS (USTS) CONTAINING PETROLEUM BY ENHANCING STATE AND LOCAL ENFORCEMENT AND RESPONSE CAPABILITY. EPA?S LUST APPROPRIATION IS PROVIDED TO 56 STATES AND TERRITORIES THROUGH COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS OR IN-KIND CONTRACTUAL ASSISTANCE FOR THE OVERSIGHT AND CLEANUP OF PETROLEUM RELEASES FROM USTS. THESE STATES AND TERRITORIES HAVE THE AUTHORITY TO RESPOND TO PETROLEUM RELEASES FROM USTS USING LUST FUNDS WHERE OWNERS AND OPERATORS ARE UNKNOWN, UNWILLING, OR UNABLE TO TAKE CORRECTIVE ACTIONS THEMSELVES. STATES MAY ALSO OVERSEE AND ENFORCE RESPONSIBLE PARTY CLEANUPS AND COST RECOVER FROM RESPONSIBLE PARTIES WHO ARE UNWILLING TO PAY FOR CLEANUPS.    EPA MAY ALSO PROVIDE IN KIND CONTRACTUAL ASSISTANCE TO RECIPIENTS OF LUST CLEANUP COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS WITH LUST TRUST FUND RESOURCES.
Definition: EPA PROVIDES FUNDING THROUGH THE LEAKING UNDERGROUND STORAGE TANKS (LUST) TRUST FUND COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS FOR OVERSIGHT, CORRECTIVE ACTION AND RELATED ENFORCEMENT ACTIVITIES ASSOCIATED WITH PETROLEUM RELEASES FROM UNDERGROUND STORAGE TANKS (USTS), AND FOR THE DEVELOPMENT AND/OR IMPLEMENTATION OF THE LUST PROGRAM IN STATES.  THE LUST TRUST FUND COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS PROVIDE ASSISTANCE TO STATES TO RESPOND TO PETROLEUM RELEASES FROM USTS WHERE OWNERS AND OPERATORS ARE UNKNOWN, UNWILLING OR UNABLE TO TAKE CORRECTIVE ACTIONS THEMSELVES, AND IN CERTAIN OTHER CIRCUMSTANCES.  STATES MAY ALSO OVERSEE RESPONSIBLE PARTY CLEANUPS.  WHEN THE LUST TRUST FUND IS USED, TANK OWNERS/OPERATORS ARE LIABLE TO THE STATE FOR COSTS INCURRED AND ARE SUBJECT TO COST RECOVERY ACTIONS.  EPA MAY ALSO PROVIDE IN KIND CONTRACTUAL ASSISTANCE TO RECIPIENTS OF LUST COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS WITH LEAKING UNDERGROUND STORAGE TANKS TRUST FUND RESOURCES.  EPA SHALL NOT REPROGRAM THESE FUNDS OUT OF THIS PROGRAM PROJECT; NOR ARE THESE FUNDS TO BE USED FOR ANY PURPOSE OTHER THAN WHAT IS DEFINED HERE.

Name: MARINE POLLUTION
Title: 88 - MARINE POLLUTION
Laws: MARINE, POLLUTION, RESEARCH SANCTUARIES ACTSHORE PROTECTION ACTALASKA CRUISE SHIP OPERATIONS ACTNATIONAL INVASIVE SPECIES ACTCLEAN WATER ACT SECTION 301(H), 312,  AND 403MARINE PLASTIC POLLUTION RESEARCH AND CONTROL ACTCLEAN BOATING ACTCOASTAL ZONE ACT REAUTHORIZATION AMENDMENTS OF 1990 (CZARA)FEDERAL INSECTICIDE, FUNGICIDE AND RODENTICIDE ACTLIBERTY SHIP ACTMARINE DEBRIS RESEARCH, PREVENTION AND REDUCTION ACT OF 2006 (MDRPRA)NATIONAL DEFENSE AUTHORIZATION ACT FOR 2004, SECTION 3516NATIONAL ENVIRONMENTAL POLICY ACT, SECTION 102NISA OF 1996NORTH AMERICAN FREE TRADE AGREEMENTOCEAN DUMPING BAN ACT OF 1988OLYMPIC AIR POLLUTION CONTROL ACTPENSION PROTECTION ACTRESOURCE CONSERVATION AND RECOVERY ACTSAFE DRINKING WATER ACTSHORE PROTECTION ACTTOXIC SUBSTANCES CONTROL ACTWATER RESOURCES DEVELOPMENT ACTWET WEATHER WATER QUALITY ACT OF 2000
Narrative: THE GOALS OF THE COASTAL PROGRAMS ARE TO ENSURE THE PROTECTION OF THE MARINE ECOSYSTEMS THROUGH ADEQUATE CONTROLS ON POINT SOURCE DISCHARGES AND DISCHARGES FROM VESSELS, MANAGING DREDGED MATERIAL AND OCEAN DUMPING, DEVELOPING REGIONAL AND INTERNATIONAL COLLABORATIONS, MONITORING OCEAN AND COASTAL WATERS AND MANAGING OTHER MARINE ISSUES SUCH AS MARINE DEBRIS, INVASIVE SPECIES, SUSTAINABLE PORTS, AND THE MARINE TRANSPORTATION SYSTEM.  MAJOR AREAS OF EFFORT INCLUDE: DEVELOPING AND IMPLEMENTING REGULATIONS AND TECHNICAL GUIDANCE TO CONTROL POLLUTANTS FROM VESSEL OPERATIONAL DISCHARGES AND ISSUING PERMITS FOR MATERIALS TO BE DUMPED IN OCEAN WATERS.DESIGNATING, MONITORING, AND MANAGING OCEAN DUMPING SITES AND IMPLEMENTING PROVISIONS OF THE NATIONAL DREDGING POLICY.PARTICIPATING IN INTERNATIONAL MARINE PROTECTION PROGRAMS WITH OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES THROUGH NEGOTIATIONS OF INTERNATIONAL STANDARDS THAT ADDRESS VESSEL-RELATED SOURCES OF AQUATIC INVASIVE SPECIES, HARMFUL ANTIFOULANTS, OPERATIONAL DISCHARGES FROM VESSELS, DUMPING OF WASTES AT SEA, AND MARINE DEBRIS. WORKING WITH A WIDE VARIETY OF STAKEHOLDERS TO DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT ECOSYSTEM-BASED MANAGEMENT TOOLS, STRATEGIES, AND PLANS FOR COASTAL ECOSYSTEMS IN ORDER TO RESTORE AND MAINTAIN THE HEALTH OF COASTAL AQUATIC COMMUNITIES ON A PRIORITY BASIS, INCLUDING PROMOTION OF DREDGED MATERIAL MANAGEMENT IN A WATERSHED CONTEXT.
Definition: IMPLEMENTATION OF REGULATORY AND SUPPORT ACTIVITIES RELATING TO OCEAN DISCHARGES AND RELATED MARINE ECOSYSTEM PROTECTION ACTIVITIES.

Name: NATIONAL ESTUARY PROGRAM / COASTAL WATERWAYS
Title: 89 - NATIONAL ESTUARY PROGRAM / COASTAL WATERWAYS
Laws: CLEAN WATER ACT, SECTIONS 320 AND 1041990 GREAT LAKES CRITICAL PROGRAMS ACT; 2002 GREAT LAKES AND LAKE CHAMPLAIN ACT; ESTUARIES AND CLEAN WATERS ACT OF 2000; PROTECTION, AND RESTORATION ACT OF 1990; NORTH AMERICAN WETLANDS CONSERVATION ACT; WATER RESOURCES DEVELOPMENT ACT; 1909 THE BOUNDARY WATERS TREATY; 1978 GLWQA; 1987 GREAT LAKES WATER QUALITY AGREEMENT; 1987 MONTREAL PROTOCOL ON OZONE DEPLETING SUBSTANCES; 1996 HABITAT AGENDA; 1997 CANADA-U.S. GREAT LAKES BI-NATIONAL TOXICS STRATEGY; COASTAL WETLANDS PLANNING.
Narrative: THE GOAL OF THIS PROGRAM IS TO RESTORE THE PHYSICAL, CHEMICAL, AND BIOLOGICAL INTEGRITY OF THE NATIONAL ESTUARIES AND COASTAL WATERSHEDS BY PROTECTING AND ENHANCING WATER QUALITY AND LIVING RESOURCES.  MAJOR AREAS OF EFFORT INCLUDE: ALIGNING NEP/COASTAL WATERWAYS POLICY WITH THE EXECUTIVE ORDER ON STEWARDSHIP OF THE OCEAN, OUR COASTS, AND THE GREAT LAKES; INTEGRATING THE NEP/COASTAL WATERWAYS PROGRAM INTO FEDERAL AGENCY IMPLEMENTATION OF THAT EXECUTIVE ORDER; MAINTAINING AND FORMING PARTNERSHIPS WITH OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES WITH RESPONSIBILITY TO IMPLEMENT THAT EXECUTIVE ORDER;SUPPORTING THE 28 NATIONAL ESTUARY PROGRAMS (NEP) THROUGH (1) CONTINUED IMPLEMENTATION OF COMPREHENSIVE CONSERVATION AND MANAGEMENT PLANS (CCMPS) AND (2) IMPLEMENTATION OF CLEAN WATER ACT (CWA) CORE PROGRAMS IN THEIR ESTUARINE ECOSYSTEMS; MONITORING AND COASTAL ASSESSMENT RESULTING IN THE CONTINUED ISSUANCE OF NATIONAL COASTAL CONDITION REPORTS;IDENTIFYING HEALTHY AND IMPAIRED WATERSHED COMPONENTS, INCLUDING SIGNIFICANT IMPAIRMENTS OUTSIDE THE AREA ADDRESSED BY THE COMPREHENSIVE CONSERVATION AND MANAGEMENT PLANS, THAT COULD AFFECT THE WATER QUALITY AND ECOLOGICAL INTEGRITY OF ESTUARIES;SUPPORTING ENHANCEMENT OF NEP CLIMATE CHANGE ADAPTATION ACTIVITIES; ANDADDRESSING THREATS LIKE HYPOXIA IN THE GULF OF MEXICO IN NON-NEP ESTUARY/COASTAL WATERSHEDS.
Definition: GRANTS AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO THE NATIONAL ESTUARY PROGRAM AND TO OTHER COASTAL WATERSHEDS.

Name: NEPA IMPLEMENTATION
Title: 90 - NEPA IMPLEMENTATION
Laws: NATIONAL ENVIRONMENTAL POLICY ACT (NEPA), 1969, CLEAN AIR ACT AS AMENDED IN 1990, CLEAN WATER ACT IN 1972, MARINE PROTECTION, RESEARCH AND SANCTUARIES ACT IN 1972,ANTARCTIC SCIENCE, TOURISM, AND CONSERVATION ACT (ASTCA) IN 1996, ENDANGERED SPECIES ACT (ESA) IN 1973, NATIONAL HISTORIC PRESERVATION ACT (NHPA) IN 1966
Narrative: THE NEPA PROGRAM SUPPORTS THIS PROGRAM/PROJECT IN ITS ENTIRETY AND PROVIDES ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION BY REVIEWING NEPA DOCUMENTS, PRIMARILY EISS, FOR ALL MAJOR FEDERAL ACTIONS WHICH HAVE A SIGNIFICANT ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT. EPA IDENTIFIES THE LIKELY ADVERSE ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACTS OF THE PROPOSED ACTIONS; RECOMMENDS ALTERNATIVES, WHERE NECESSARY, TO MITIGATE THE IMPACTS; AND NEGOTIATES WITH OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES TO PREVENT/REMEDY THE POTENTIAL ADVERSE ENVIRONMENTAL CONSEQUENCES OF THESE ACTIONS.  THROUGH EARLY INVOLVEMENT IN THE PROJECT SCOPING PROCESS; PROMOTING METHODS AND TRAINING FOR ENGAGING FEDERAL, STATE, LOCAL AND TRIBAL PARTNERS TO DEVELOP COLLABORATION SKILLS AND SUCCESSFUL COLLABORATIVE AGREEMENTS APPLICABLE TO VARIOUS SITUATIONS AND STAGES OF THE NEPA PROCESS; DEVELOPING TRAINING FOR THE PUBLIC ON NEPA REQUIREMENTS AND EFFECTIVE PUBLIC INVOLVEMENT; AND INTEGRATING THE NEPA PROCESS WITH ENVIRONMENTAL MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS. EPA CONTINUES TO MODERNIZE AND IMPROVE THE NEPA PROCESS AND OUR PARTNERSHIP WITH THE OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES.ENSURE EPA DEVELOPS A SOLID PROGRAM OF COMPLIANCE WITH NEPA, OTHER APPLICABLE STATUTES, AND NEPA-RELATED ENVIRONMENTAL JUSTICE REQUIREMENTS. (HQ)MANAGE THE OFFICIAL FILING ACTIVITY FOR ALL FEDERAL ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT STATEMENTS (EIS) IN ACCORDANCE WITH A MEMORANDUM OF UNDERSTANDING WITH THE COUNCIL ON ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY. (HQ)ENSURE PREPARATION OF ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENTS OF U.S. BASED NONGOVERNMENTAL ACTIVITIES IN ANTARCTICA, PRIMARILY TOURISM. (HQ)ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL TRADE AGREEMENTS. (HQ)REVIEW MAJOR FEDERAL ACTIONS (PROJECTS AND PROGRAMS) TO DETERMINE THEIR ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACTS AND RECOMMEND STEPS TO AVOID AND MITIGATE FOR ADVERSE EFFECTS. (HQ, RT)FOSTER COOPERATION WITH OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES TO ENSURE COMPLIANCE WITH APPLICABLE ENVIRONMENTAL LAWS AND TO BETTER INTEGRATE POLLUTION PREVENTION AND ECOLOGICAL RISK ASSESSMENT INTO THEIR PROGRAMS. (HQ, RT)FOSTER ECOSYSTEM PROTECTION AND THE DEVELOPMENT OF SOUND DATA AND METHODOLOGIES WHICH ASSESS ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT AND ECOLOGICAL RISKS. (HQ, RT)TARGET HIGH IMPACT FEDERAL PROGRAM ACTIONS, SUCH AS THOSE AFFECTING WATER RESOURCES AND FEDERAL ENERGY/TRANSPORTATION RELATED PROJECTS. USING INTERAGENCY WORKING GROUPS, POLICIES AND SPECIFIC PROJECT REVIEWS, STRENGTHEN THE PROTECTION OF ECOLOGICAL RESOURCES AND MITIGATE ADVERSE IMPACTS TO TERRESTRIAL AND AQUATIC HABITATS. (HQ, RT)PREPARE ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEWS (EISS OR EAS) FOR EPA-ISSUED NEW SOURCE NATIONAL POLLUTANT DISCHARGE ELIMINATION SYSTEM (NPDES) PERMITS WHERE A STATE/TRIBE HAS NOT ASSUMED THE NPDES PROGRAM, FOR OFF-SHORE OIL AND GAS SOURCES, EPA LABORATORIES AND FACILITIES, AND FOR CLEAN WATER ACT WASTEWATER TREATMENT PLANT GRANTS. (HQ, RT)PREPARE ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEWS (EISS OR EAS) FOR SPECIAL APPROPRIATION GRANTS FOR WASTEWATER, WATER SUPPLY AND SOLID WASTE COLLECTION FACILITIES; US/MEXICO BORDER ENVIRONMENT COOPERATION COMMISSION PROJECTS; AND, REVIEWS CONDUCTED UNDER THE ?VOLUNTARY NEPA POLICY.? (HQ, RT)
Definition: THE NEPA PROGRAM PROVIDES ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION BY MEETING ITS STATUTORY OBLIGATION TO REVIEW ALL ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT STATEMENTS (EISS). EISS ARE REQUIRED FOR MAJOR FEDERAL ACTIONS WHICH HAVE A SIGNIFICANT ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT. EPA FOCUSES ON ADVERSE ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACTS OF PROPOSED ACTIONS AND RECOMMENDS MEASURES, TO MITIGATE THEM. EPA WORKS COLLABORATIVELY WITH OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES TO AVOID ADVERSE ENVIRONMENTAL CONSEQUENCES OF PROPOSED ACTIONS AND TO ENSURE TIMELY COMPLETION OF THE NEPA PROCESS. THE NEPA PROGRAM ALSO PREPARES EISS AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASSESSMENTS FOR EPA ACTIVITIES AND ASSURES THAT THEY ARE SUPPORTED BY SOUND ANALYSIS AND ADDRESS MITIGATION OF ADVERSE IMPACTS.

Name: OIL SPILL: PREVENTION, PREPAREDNESS AND RESPONSE
Title: 91 - OIL SPILL: PREVENTION, PREPAREDNESS AND RESPONSE
Laws: SECTION 311 OF THE FEDERAL WATER POLLUTION CONTROL ACT (1972) AS AMENDED BY SECTION 4202 OF THE OIL POLLUTION ACT OF 1990 (OPA).
Narrative: CONDUCT NATIONAL REGULATORY, POLICY AND PROGRAM MANAGEMENT ACTIVITIES TO LEAD THE OIL SPILL PREVENTION, PREPAREDNESS, AND RESPONSE PROGRAMS.PROVIDE NATIONAL POLICY DIRECTION, MANAGEMENT, AND OVERSIGHT OF OIL SPILL RESPONSE ACTIVITIES TO REGIONS SO THEY MAY CONDUCT TECHNICALLY-EFFICIENT AND COST-EFFECTIVE RESPONSES.  SUPPORTS FIELD OPERATIONS THROUGH OPERATIONAL GUIDANCE, REGULATIONS, TECHNICAL BULLETINS.  CONDUCT OIL STORAGE FACILITY INSPECTIONS WHICH INCLUDE SITE VISITS, SPCC PLAN REVIEW, WORKING WITH FACILITIES TO DEMONSTRATE HOW SPCC REQUIREMENTS APPLY SPECIFICALLY TO FACILITY DESIGN AND OPERATIONS, AND FOLLOW-UP CHECKS TO ENSURE COMPLIANCE WITH EPA?S SPCC REGULATION. CONDUCT PERIODIC REVIEWS TO APPROVE, IF APPROPRIATE, FRPS FOR ALL FACILITIES THAT POSE A THREAT OF SIGNIFICANT AND SUBSTANTIAL HARM TO THE ENVIRONMENT TO ENSURE THEY WILL BE ADEQUATELY PREPARED TO RESPOND TO AN OIL SPILL.  REGIONAL ON-SCENE COORDINATORS LEAD AREA COMMITTEES WHICH DEVELOP AREA CONTINGENCY PLANS TO ADDRESS THE REMOVAL OF A WORST CASE DISCHARGE OF OIL, TO MITIGATE OR PREVENT A SUBSTANTIAL THREAT OF SUCH A DISCHARGE FOR FACILITIES IN THE INLAND ZONE, AND TO RESPOND TO OIL SPILLS IN THE INLAND ZONES AS NECESSARY.  THE AREA COMMITTEES PUBLISH UPDATES TO THESE PLANS IN EACH OF THE 13 ORIGINALLY-DESIGNATED AREAS AND CONDUCT AREA COMMITTEE MEETINGS.FUNDS PROVIDED UNDER THIS PROGRAM/PROJECT MAY BE UTILIZED TO OBTAIN SEE PROGRAM RESOURCES TO SUPPORT EPA HEADQUARTERS AND/OR REGIONAL OFFICES UNDER THE AUTHORITY OF THE "ENVIRONMENTAL PROGRAMS ASSISTANCE ACT" PUBLIC LAW 98-313, JUNE 12, 1984.
Definition: THE PROGRAM INCLUDES OIL SPILL PREVENTION, PREPAREDNESS, AND RESPONSE ACTIVITIES.  THE SPILL PREVENTION, CONTROL, AND COUNTERMEASURES (SPCC) SERVE TO PREVENT OIL SPILLS THAT HAVE POTENTIAL TO REACH NAVIGABLE WATERS.  AREA PLANNING ENSURES THAT EPA AND OTHER RESPONSE ORGANIZATIONS HAVE IDENTIFIED RESOURCES THAT NEED TO BE PROTECTED, AND METHODS TO EFFECTIVELY COORDINATE DURING RESPONSE.  THE FACILITY RESPONSE PLAN (FRP) REQUIRES THE LARGEST OIL FACILITIES TO HAVE CAPABILITY TO RESPOND TO A WORST-CASE SPILL, AND EPA?S SPILL RESPONSE PROGRAM HAS AUTHORITIES AND RESOURCES TO LEAD OR OVERSEE OIL SPILL RESPONSE ACTIVITIES.

Name: POLLUTION PREVENTION PROGRAM
Title: 95 - POLLUTION PREVENTION PROGRAM
Laws: POLLUTION PREVENTION ACT (PPA)TOXIC SUBSTANCES CONTROL ACT (TSCA)RESOURCE CONSERVATION AND RECOVERY ACT (RCRA)  8001
Narrative: EPA CONTINUOUSLY SEEKS NEW INNOVATIVE APPROACHES FOR PREVENTING POLLUTION AT THE SOURCE AND IMPLEMENTS THOSE APPROACHES THROUGH VARIOUS MECHANISMS SUCH AS CHALLENGES AND INCENTIVES (E.G. RECOGNITION).  EXAMPLES INCLUDE THE GREEN SUPPLIERS NETWORK, HOSPITALS FOR A HEALTHY ENVIRONMENT AND P2 INFORMATION SHARING, ENVIRONMENTAL MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS AND OTHER P2 PROJECTS.GREEN CHEMISTRY:  THE GREEN CHEMISTRY PROGRAM PROMOTES THE RESEARCH, DEVELOPMENT, AND IMPLEMENTATION OF INNOVATIVE CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGIES THAT ELIMINATE OR REDUCE HAZARDOUS SUBSTANCES DURING THE DESIGN, MANUFACTURE, AND USE OF CHEMICAL PRODUCTS AND PROCESSES.GREEN ENGINEERING PROGRAM:  THE GOALS OF THE GREEN ENGINEERING (GE) PROGRAM ARE TWOFOLD: TO INCORPORATE GREEN OR ENVIRONMENTALLY-CONSCIOUS THINKING AND APPROACHES INTO THE ACADEMIC AND INDUSTRIAL COMMUNITIES REGARDING THE DESIGN, COMMERCIALIZATION AND USE OF PROCESSES AND PRODUCTS; AND TO PROMOTE AND FOSTER DEVELOPMENT AND COMMERCIALIZATION OF GREEN ENGINEERING APPROACHES AND TECHNOLOGIES. DESIGN FOR THE ENVIRONMENT (DFE):  EPA?S DESIGN FOR THE ENVIRONMENT (DFE) PROGRAM WORKS IN PARTNERSHIP WITH INDUSTRY SECTORS TO DEVELOP COMPARATIVE RISK, PERFORMANCE, AND COST INFORMATION ABOUT ALTERNATIVE TECHNOLOGIES, CHEMICALS, AND PROCESSES TO BETTER AID INDUSTRY IN MAKING ENVIRONMENTALLY INFORMED DECISIONS.
Definition: PROGRAM TO PREVENT POLLUTANTS FROM BEING GENERATED AT THE SOURCE BY PROMOTING ENVIRONMENTALLY SOUND BUSINESS PRACTICES AND THE DEVELOPMENT OF SAFER (GREEN) CHEMICALS, TECHNOLOGIES AND PROCESSES.

Name: RADIATION:  PROTECTION
Title: 97 - RADIATION:  PROTECTION
Laws: AEA OF 1954, AS AMENDED, 42 U.S.C. 2011 ET SEQ. (1970), AND REORGANIZATION PLAN #3 OF 1970; CAA AMENDMENTS OF 1990; CERCLA AS AMENDED BY THE SARA OF 1986; ENERGY POLICY ACT OF 1992, P.L. 102-486; EXECUTIVE ORDER 12241 OF SEPTEMBER 1980, NATIONAL CONTINGENCY PLAN, 3 CFR, 1980; NWPA OF 1982; PHSA AS AMENDED, 42 U.S.C. 201 ET SEQ.; SDWA; URANIUM  MILL TAILINGS RADIATION CONTROL ACT (UMTRCA) OF 1978; WIPP LAND WITHDRAWAL ACT.
Narrative: FUNDS UNDER THIS PROGRAM PROJECT MAY BE USED FOR SEE SUPPORT.EPM - HQPROVIDE ADVICE AND COUNSEL TO THE ADMINISTRATOR ON THE RADIOLOGICAL WASTE MANAGEMENT PROGRAMS.PROVIDE EFFECTIVE POLICY, PROGRAM, AND MANAGEMENT GUIDANCE TO THE OFFICE OF AIR AND RADIATION PROGRAM, STAFF, AND EPA REGIONAL OFFICES ON RADIATION PROGRAMS.PROVIDE ANALYSIS, PLANNING, BUDGETING, AND MANAGEMENT CAPABILITY TO ENSURE ANALYTIC SUPPORT TO REGIONAL, STATE, AND TRIBAL PROGRAMS ON RADIATION PROGRAMS.DEVELOP RADIATION RISK MANAGEMENT POLICY, STANDARDS, AND GUIDANCE FOR OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES AND STATES. MAINTAIN CERTIFICATION AND OVERSIGHT RESPONSIBILITIES FOR DOE WASTE DISPOSAL ACTIVITIES AT THE WIPP INCLUDING SITE INSPECTIONS AT WASTE GENERATOR SITES, NATIONAL LABORATORIES, AND THE WIPP FACILITY; REVIEW OF CHANGES TO THE CERTIFICATION; MODIFICATION TO THE CERTIFICATION THROUGH RULEMAKING; AND PERIODIC (EVERY 5 YEARS) RECERTIFICATION.INSPECT WASTE CHARACTERIZATION PROCESSES AND QUALITY ASSURANCE PROGRAMS AT THE DEPARTMENT OF ENERGY' WIPP FACILITY, SUPPORTING NATIONAL LABORATORIES, CONTRACTORS AND NUMEROUS WASTE GENERATOR SITES. ISSUE SUBSEQUENT REPORTS AND APPROVALS AS APPROPRIATE.DEVELOP WASTE MANAGEMENT GUIDANCE OR REGULATIONS TO FACILITATE THE DISPOSAL OF LOW-ACTIVITY MIXED WASTE WHICH INCORPORATES THE INNOVATIVE APPROACH OF COMBINING EXISTING RCRA REQUIREMENTS WITH TRADITIONAL RADIOLOGICAL WASTE MANAGEMENT COMPONENTS.GUIDANCE OR STANDARDS AS APPROPRIATE ON URANIUM MINING, WHICH INCLUDED TECHNOLOGICALLY ENHANCED NATURALLY-OCCURRING RADIOACTIVE MATERIAL (TENORM).IMPLEMENT THE TENORM STRATEGY, AND CONTINUE TO WORK WITH STATES ON ISSUES INVOLVING THE REGULATION AND DISPOSAL OF NATURALLY-OCCURRING RADIOACTIVE MATERIAL. EPM - RTWORK WITH OTHER STATES ON MINING LEGACY WASTE DISPOSAL ISSUESPROVIDE INFORMATION ON RADIATION IN THE ENVIRONMENT AND ENSURE IT IS AVAILABLE AND EASILY ACCESSIBLE TO THE GENERAL PUBLIC. ENSURE RADIATION INFORMATION IS ACCESSIBLE ON THE AGENCY'S ENVIROFACTS INTERNET SITE TO INTEGRATE RADIATION INFORMATION WITH CERCLA, RCRA, SDWIS, AND AIRS.PROVIDE POLICY AND TECHNICAL ADVICE TO THE OFFICE OF AIR AND RADIATION.S&T - HQPROVIDE RADIATION DATA AND ANALYSIS SUPPORT TO THE OFFICE OF AIR AND RADIATION, EPA REGIONAL OFFICE AND OTHER EPA OFFICES.OPERATE AND MAINTAIN THE NATIONAL AIR AND RADIATION ENVIRONMENTAL LABORATORY (NAREL), LOCATED IN MONTGOMERY, ALABAMA AND THE RADIATION AND INDOOR ENVIRONMENTS NATIONAL LABORATORY (R&IE) IN LAS VEGAS, NEVADA.SF - HQPROVIDE ON-SITE TECHNICAL AND LABORATORY SUPPORT FOR MIXED-WASTE ISSUES THAT ARE SUPERFUND-RELATED.
Definition: THIS PROGRAM INCLUDES ACTIVITIES FOR RADIATION CLEAN UP; FEDERAL GUIDANCE; RISK MODELING; WASTE ISOLATION PILOT PLANT (WIPP); RADIATION AIR TOXICS; NATURALLY-OCCURRING RADIOACTIVE MATERIAL; RADIATION WASTE MANAGEMENT; AND RADIOACTIVE AND MIXED WASTE OPERATIONS AND MEASUREMENTS.  ALSO INCLUDES RADIATION LAB-RELATED INFRASTRUCTURE EXPENSES.

Name: RADIATION:  RESPONSE PREPAREDNESS
Title: 98 - RADIATION:  RESPONSE PREPAREDNESS
Laws: ATOMIC ENERGY ACT (AEA) OF 1954, AS AMENDED, 42 U.S.C. 2011 ET SEQ. (1970), AND REORGANIZATION PLAN #3 OF 1970; CLEAN AIR ACT (CAA) AMENDMENTS OF 1990; COMPREHENSIVE ENVIRONMENTAL RESPONSE, COMPENSATION, AND LIABILITY ACT (CERCLA); NATIONAL OIL AND HAZARDOUS SUBSTANCES POLLUTION CONTINGENCY PLAN (NCP), 40 CFR 300; EXECUTIVE ORDER 12241 OF SEPTEMBER 1980, NATIONAL CONTINGENCY PLAN, 3 CFR, 1980; EXECUTIVE ORDER 12656 OF NOVEMBER 1988, ASSIGNMENT OF EMERGENCY PREPAREDNESS RESPONSIBILITIES, 3 CFR, 1988; HOMELAND SECURITY ACT OF 2002; POST-KATRINA EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT REFORM ACT OF 2006 (PKEMRA); PUBLIC HEALTH SERVICE ACT (PHSA), AS AMENDED, 42 U.S.C. 201 ET SEQ.; ROBERT T. (NOTE: SOURCE: FY12 CJ AND FY13 PB FACT SHEETS).

THIS INCLUDES CONDUCTING PROTECTIVE ACTION GUIDE WORKSHOPS AND RADIOLOGICAL EMERGENCY RESPONSE EXERCISES. RESPOND TO EMERGENCY INCIDENTS. PROVIDE POLICY DEVELOPMENT, ASSET MANAGEMENT, AND PLAN INTRA-AGENCY COORDINATION, AND FIELD EXERCISES FOR EPA?S COUNTER-TERRORISM PROGRAM.MAINTAIN AND UPDATE PROTECTIVE ACTION GUIDES (PAG) FOR USE BY FEDERAL, STATE AND LOCAL OFFICIALS, AND PROVIDE TRAINING ON THE USE OF THE PAGS.PROVIDE INFORMATION TO THE PUBLIC ON EPA EMERGENCY RESPONSE ACTIVITIES AND CAPABILITIES.COORDINATE WITH PARTNERS UNDER THE FEDERAL RADIOLOGICAL PREPAREDNESS COORDINATING COMMITTEE TO REVISE FEDERAL RADIATION EMERGENCY RESPONSE PLANS AND DEVELOP RADIOLOGICAL EMERGENCY RESPONSE PROTOCOLS AND STANDARDS.COORDINATE WITH THE INTERNATIONAL ATOMIC ENERGY AGENCY, OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES INCLUDING NRC AND DOE, AND STATES TO DEVELOP MECHANISMS FOR CONTROLLING TRANSBOUNDARY MOVEMENT OF RADIOACTIVE MATERIALS.EPM - RTPROVIDE ON-SITE TECHNICAL SUPPORT TO STATE RADIATION, SOLID WASTE AND HEALTH PROGRAMS THAT REGULATE RADIATION REMEDIATION, PARTICIPATE IN THE PROTECTIVE ACTION GUIDELINES WORKSHOPS AND RADIOLOGICAL EMERGENCY RESPONSE EXERCISES.SERVE AS A LIAISON WITH LOCAL STAKEHOLDERS AND PUBLIC TO PROVIDE INFORMATION REGARDING ONSITE EMERGENCY RESPONSE ACTIVITIES AND CAPABILITIES.PROVIDE ASSISTANCE WHERE REQUIRED IN SUPPORT OF THE ORPHAN SOURCES INITIATIVE.PROVIDE POLICY AND TECHNICAL ADVICE TO THE OFFICE OF AIR AND RADIATION.S&T - HQPROVIDE DATA AND ANALYSIS SUPPORT TO THE OFFICE OF AIR AND RADIATION PROGRAM AND EPA REGIONAL OFFICES ON RADIATION EMERGENCY RESPONSE PROGRAMS.PARTICIPATE IN AND PROVIDE ON-SITE TECHNICAL SUPPORT TO EPA?S COUNTER-TERRORISM PROGRAM.MAINTAIN READINESS FOR RADIOLOGICAL EMERGENCY RESPONSES WHICH INCLUDES PARTICIPATION IN EMERGENCY RESPONSE EXERCISES.OPERATE AND MAINTAIN THE NATIONAL ENVIRONMENTAL RADIOLOGICAL MONITORING SYSTEM.FUND OPERATION OF THE NATIONAL AIR AND RADIATION ENVIRONMENTAL LABORATORY (NAREL), LOCATED IN MONTGOMERY, ALABAMA AND THE RADIATION AND INDOOR ENVIRONMENTS NATIONAL LABORATORY (R&IE) IN LAS VEGAS, NEVADA.
Definition: THIS PROGRAM INCLUDES FEDERAL PREPAREDNESS ACTIVITIES INCLUDING RADIATION EMERGENCY RESPONSE TEAM AND EQUIPMENT, TRAINING AND OUTREACH, AND RADIOLOGICAL EMERGENCY RESPONSE GUIDANCE.  ALSO INCLUDE RADIATION LAB RELATED INFRASTRUCTURE EXPENSES.

Name: RCRA:  CORRECTIVE ACTION
Title: 99 - RCRA:  CORRECTIVE ACTION
Laws: SOLID WASTE DISPOSAL ACT (1965), AS AMENDED BY THE RESOURCE CONSERVATION AND RECOVERY ACT (1976), 42 UNITED STATES CODE (U.S.C.). 6901 ET SEQ. ? SECTIONS 3004, 3005, 8001AND THE TOXIC SUBSTANCE CONTROL ACT (1976), 15 U.S.C. 2605 ET SEQ. ? SECTION 6.
Narrative: CONTROLLING CONTAMINANT RELEASES AND HUMAN EXPOSURES IS A CONTINUATION OF CURRENT ACTIVITIES UNDER THE RCRA CORRECTIVE ACTION PROGRAM AND IS REQUIRED BY LAW.  THE ENVIRONMENTAL INDICATORS (EI) HUMAN EXPOSURES CONTROLLED AND GROUNDWATER RELEASES CONTROLLED SHOW RESULTS THAT ARE ACHIEVED THROUGH INTERIM MEASURES SUCH AS STABILIZATION ACTIVITIES OR THROUGH A FINAL REMEDY.  ALTHOUGH THESE RESULTS ARE IMPORTANT MILESTONES FOR CLEANUP PROGRAMS, ACHIEVING THESE RESULTS DOES NOT IMPLY THAT THE LONG-TERM RISKS ASSOCIATED WITH CONTAMINATION AT THE FACILITY ARE CONTROLLED OR THAT A FACILITY IS CLEANED UP.  THESE ARE PRIMARILY INTERIM STABILIZATION MEASURES. EVEN AFTER ACHIEVING THESE MILESTONES, FACILITIES ARE REQUIRED TO CONTINUE WITH STABILIZATION AND/OR REMEDY IMPLEMENTATION UNTIL THE FINAL CLEANUP STANDARDS HAVE BEEN MET.  ENVIRONMENTAL INDICATORS, SUCH AS HUMAN EXPOSURES CONTROLLED AND GROUNDWATER RELEASES CONTROLLED, REPRESENT NEW WAYS OF MEASURING INTERIM SUCCESS IN A PROGRAM.  KEY ACTIVITIES THROUGH WHICH THE PROGRAM IS IMPLEMENTED INCLUDE: RULEMAKING AND DEVELOPMENT OF TECHNICAL AND PROGRAM IMPLEMENTATION POLICIES AND GUIDANCE; PROGRAM OVERSIGHT AT NATIONAL, REGIONAL, AND STATE LEVELS; AND CONDUCTING CORRECTIVE ACTION ACTIVITIES INCLUDING ASSESSMENTS, INVESTIGATIONS, STABILIZATION MEASURES, REMEDY SELECTION, REMEDY CONSTRUCTION/IMPLEMENTATION, AND LONG-TERM STEWARDSHIP.THE RCRA CORRECTIVE ACTION PROGRAM ALSO IMPLEMENTS THE TOXIC SUBSTANCES CONTROL ACT (TSCA) PROGRAM UNDER SECTION 761 TO REDUCE POLYCHLORINATED BIPHENYL (PCB) EXPOSURE FROM IMPROPER DISPOSAL AND SPILLS THROUGH CLEANUPS.  THIS AUTHORITY IS NOT DELEGATED TO THE STATES.THE FOLLOWING IS A DETAILED LIST OF MAJOR ACTIVITIES SUPPORTING THIS OBJECTIVE; THE LIST INDICATES WHETHER THE ACTIVITY IS CONDUCTED IN THE HEADQUARTERS OR REGIONAL OFFICES OR BOTH. HEADQUARTERS ONLYDEVELOP NATIONAL CORRECTIVE ACTION GUIDANCE AND REGULATIONS.ISSUE GUIDANCE ON SAFE CLEANUP AND DISPOSAL OF PCB WASTES.IMPLEMENT NATIONAL CORRECTIVE ACTION TRAINING.HEADQUARTERS AND REGIONSENHANCE PUBLIC PARTICIPATION IN SIGNIFICANT STABILIZATION ACTIONS.INFORMATION MANAGEMENT.TEST AND ASSESS CONSTRUCTION MATERIALS CONTAINING POTENTIALLY HAZARDOUS BY-PRODUCTS.REVIEW PCB CLEANUPS UNDER TSCA RISK-BASED APPROVALS.REGIONS ONLYASSESSMENTS OF RCRA FACILITIES STATUTORILY REQUIRED TO CONDUCT CORRECTIVE ACTION (CA) AND THOSE SUBJECT TO CA.INVESTIGATIONS OF RCRA FACILITIES WHERE CA HAS BEEN IMPOSED.IMPLEMENTATION OF STABILIZATION MEASURES AND DETERMINATION IF THE OBJECTIVES OF FINAL REMEDIES HAVE BEEN MET.DETERMINATION IF HUMAN EXPOSURES AND GROUNDWATER RELEASES ARE CONTROLLED.SELECTION OF FINAL REMEDY.IMPLEMENTATION OF FINAL REMEDY, CONTRACT MANAGEMENT.STATE/REGION WORK-SHARING.REGIONAL TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO STATES.OVERSIGHT OF RCRA FACILITIES WHERE CA HAS BEEN IMPOSED.GRANTS MANAGEMENT.PUBLIC PARTICIPATION/COMMUNITY RELATIONS.STATE AND LOCAL PROGRAM DEVELOPMENT.ADDRESS CLEANUPS OF PCB-CONTAMINATED MATERIALS, SUCH AS CAULK IN SCHOOLS, OFFICE BUILDINGS AND AIRPORTS.
Definition: RISKS TO HUMAN HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT AT CONTAMINATED SITES WILL BE CONTROLLED THROUGH CLEANUP, ASSESSMENT, STABILIZATION, OR OTHER ACTION AND MAKE LAND AVAILABLE FOR REUSE.  THIS PROGRAM PROVIDES CLEANUP AT INDUSTRIAL FACILITIES.  STRATEGIC TARGETS INCLUDE SITE ASSESSMENTS.  IN ADDITION, LAND WILL BE AVAILABLE FOR REUSE THROUGH CLEANUP, ASSESSMENT, STABILIZATION, OR OTHER ACTION WHICH INDICATES THAT SUCH LANDS ARE RESTORED TO LEVELS THAT ARE PROTECTIVE FOR THE NEXT REASONABLY ANTICIPATED FUTURE LAND USE.

Name: RCRA:  WASTE MANAGEMENT
Title: A1 - RCRA:  WASTE MANAGEMENT
Laws: SOLID WASTE DISPOSAL ACT (1965), AS AMENDED BY THE RESOURCE CONSERVATION AND RECOVERY ACT (1976), 42 UNITED STATES CODE (U.S.C.) 6901 ET SEQ. ? SECTIONS 3004, 3005, 8001 AND THE TOXIC SUBSTANCECONTROL ACT (1976), 15 U.S.C. 2605 ET SEQ. ? SECTION 6.
Narrative: EPA IS REQUIRED BY RCRA TO PROMULGATE REGULATIONS, DEVELOP PERMITTING STANDARDS, AND ISSUE PERMITS WITH OUR STATE PARTNERS TO ENSURE THE SAFE MANAGEMENT OF HAZARDOUS WASTE.  THE RCRA PROGRAM SIGNIFICANTLY REDUCES THE RISK OF HUMAN HEALTH AND ENVIRONMENTAL EXPOSURES TO DANGEROUS HAZARDOUS WASTES BY ESTABLISHING A COMPREHENSIVE MANAGEMENT FRAMEWORK.  THE SAFE OPERATION AND CLOSURE OF HAZARDOUS WASTE MANAGEMENT FACILITIES IS ONE OF THE MAIN FOCUSES OF EPA AND STATE RCRA PROGRAMS.  FINAL PERMIT ACTION BY THE APPROVED PERMITTING AUTHORITY (EITHER STATES OR EPA REGIONS) GENERALLY IS NECESSARY FOR MEETING THIS OBJECTIVE.  ALTHOUGH ALL OPERATING LAND DISPOSAL FACILITIES ARE PERMITTED, ALL PERMITS MUST BE REVIEWED EVERY FIVE YEARS.  PERMIT MODIFICATIONS CONTINUE TO BE ISSUED, AND MANY STORAGE AND TREATMENT FACILITIES STILL REQUIRE PERMITS TO BE ISSUED.SAFE MANAGEMENT OF HAZARDOUS WASTE STARTS WITH AN EFFECTIVE IDENTIFICATION SYSTEM TO DETERMINE WHAT WASTES ARE HAZARDOUS AND HOW THEY SHOULD BE MANAGED.  THE RISKS TO HUMAN HEALTH AND TO THE ENVIRONMENT OF UNCONTROLLED OR MISMANAGED HAZARDOUS WASTES ARE SUBSTANTIAL.  THE PRIMARY ROUTE TO EXPOSURES TO THE GENERAL PUBLIC HAS LONG BEEN TRACED THROUGH GROUNDWATER BUT EMERGING INFORMATION ON THE IMPACTS OF  BIOACCUMULATION AS WELL AS AIR DEGRADATION HAVE EXTENDED THE RANGE OF FATE AND TRANSPORT UNDER CONSIDERATION.  THE REGULATORY PROGRAM SEEKS TO IDENTIFY AND ADDRESS THE HIGHEST RISK WASTES BUT ALSO TO DESIGN COST-EFFECTIVE REGULATORY APPROACHES.THERE ARE TWO MAJOR GROUPS OF REGULATED ENTITIES NOT SPECIFICALLY TARGETED BY THIS OBJECTIVE: GENERATORS AND TRANSPORTERS.  WHILE THESE ENTITIES DO NOT RECEIVE RCRA PERMITS, THEY ARE CRITICAL IN PROPER WASTE MANAGEMENT.  THE RCRA PROGRAM, ESPECIALLY THE STATE IMPLEMENTERS, DEDICATE A SIGNIFICANT AMOUNT OF TIME AND RESOURCES TO WORK WITH GENERATORS AND TRANSPORTERS THROUGH REGULATIONS, OUTREACH, TRAINING, TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE, AND GUIDANCE TO FACILITATE COMPLIANCE.  ONGOING PROJECTS IN THE RCRA WASTE IDENTIFICATION PROGRAM SEEK TO IMPROVE SCIENTIFIC MODELING TO MORE ACCURATELY ASSESS RISK THROUGH MULTI-PATHWAYS, IMPROVEMENTS TO THE LEACHING PROCEDURES MODEL,  SEVERAL LISTINGS, AND THE IMPLEMENTATION OF THE LAND DISPOSAL RESTRICTIONS PROGRAM. MOREOVER, EPA CONTINUES TO EVALUATE THE GENERATOR PROGRAM AND TO CONSIDER REGULATORY OR GUIDANCE UPDATES TO DEAL WITH EVOLVING WASTE MANAGEMENT ISSUES.EFFORTS IN THIS OBJECTIVE ALSO ADDRESS THE DEVELOPMENT AND IMPLEMENTATION OF REGULATIONS, POLICIES, AND PROGRAMS THAT SUPPORT AND ENCOURAGE THE SAFE RECYCLING OF HAZARDOUS WASTE AND THE SAFE AND BENEFICIAL USE OF INDUSTRIAL MATERIALS ("CRADLE-TO-CRADLE"). ONE OF THE MAJOR TASKS IS TO REVIEW EXISTING INFORMATION ON HAZARDOUS WASTE RECYCLING WITH A GOAL OF DEVELOPING ACTIONS TO INCREASE RECYCLING RATES IN AREAS WHERE CURRENT RECYCLING RATES ARE LOW OR ARE DIMINISHING.  THE DEFINITION OF SOLID WASTE REPRESENTS THE REGULATORY FRAMEWORK FOR HAZARDOUS WASTE RECYCLING.  THE AGENCY, THROUGH ITS REGULATORY REFORM EFFORTS, IS ALSO EXAMINING OTHER OPPORTUNITIES TO ELIMINATE DISINCENTIVES TO SAFE HAZARDOUS WASTE RECYCLING.  THESE INCLUDE REDUCING THE BURDEN FOR IMPLEMENTING HAZARDOUS WASTE MANIFEST REQUIREMENTS (SUCH AS BY DEVELOPING A FEDERAL ELECTRONIC HAZARDOUS WASTE MANIFEST SYSTEM, OR E-MANIFEST), AND IDENTIFYING AND EVALUATING REGULATORY POLICIES FOR HAZARDOUS WASTE STREAMS AND CHEMICALS COMMONLY FOUND IN INDUSTRY THAT HAVE THE POTENTIAL TO BE RECYCLED.THE WASTE MANAGEMENT PROGRAM IMPLEMENTS THE POLYCHLORINATED BIPHENYL (PCB) PROGRAM IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE TOXIC SUBSTANCES CONTROL ACT (TSCA) BY ISSUING PCB DISPOSAL APPROVALS (E.G., FOR DEMONSTRATIONS OF NEW TECHNOLOGIES, FOR TSD OPERATIONS, AND FOR R&D ACTIVITIES) AND PROVIDING LEADERSHIP AND EXPERTISE.  THESE APPROVALS ARE WRITTEN BY EPA, AND NOT DELEGATED TO STATES.  HEADQUARTERS ONLYIMPROVE SCIENTIFIC MODELING TO MORE ACCURATELY ASSESS RISK THROUGH MULTIPLE PATHWAYS.IDENTIFY GAPS IN THE TOXICITY CHARACTERISTICS DEFINITIONS.LIST ADDITIONAL WASTES.PROMULGATE THE RCRA POST-CLOSURE RULE, WHICH WILL ALLOW CLOSURE OF CERTAIN REGULATED UNITS AS PART OF CORRECTIVE ACTION RATHER THAN THROUGH SEPARATE PERMITS.REVIEW THE CURRENT CATEGORIZATION OF WASTE STREAMS AND DETERMINE THE NEEDED CONTROLS TO PREVENT ENVIRONMENTAL HARM; REVIEW DATA ON NEW AND EMERGING WASTE STREAMS TO DETERMINE APPROPRIATE REGULATORY APPROACHES.INCREASE THE NUMBER OF ANALYTICAL AND SAMPLING METHODS AVAILABLE TO WASTE PROGRAMS TO ENSURE THE PROPER MEASUREMENT OF ENVIRONMENTAL RELEASES TO ALL MEDIA.DEVELOP NEW COSTING ANALYSES AND OTHER DOCUMENTATION TO SUPPORT LONG-TERM IMPLEMENTATION OF A FEDERAL E-MANIFEST SYSTEM.REDUCE THE AGGREGATE REPORTING AND RECORD KEEPING BURDEN ON REGULATED ENTITIES THROUGH USE OF ELECTRONIC REPORTING WHERE POSSIBLE, WHILE AT THE SAME TIME IMPROVING TRANSPARENCY AND PUBLIC ACCESS TO INFORMATION.ESTABLISH/UPDATE LANDFILL CRITERIA. DEVELOP REGULATIONS, POLICIES, AND GUIDANCE ASSOCIATED WITH THE DEFINITION OF SOLID WASTE THAT ADDRESS PARTICULAR HAZARDOUS WASTE RECYCLING ISSUES AND PROBLEMS AND THAT ELIMINATE THE DISINCENTIVES TO THE SAFE RECYCLING OF HAZARDOUS WASTES.DEVELOP REGULATIONS AND POLICIES OTHER THAN THE DEFINITION OF SOLID WASTE, SUCH AS THE LAND DISPOSAL RESTRICTIONS PROGRAM AND THE BENEFICIAL USE OF INDUSTRIAL MATERIALS PROGRAM, THAT CREATE INCENTIVES FOR ENVIRONMENTALLY SOUND HAZARDOUS WASTE RECYCLING.COLLECT PERTINENT DATA THROUGH NATIONAL OR INDUSTRY-SPECIFIC SURVEYS TO MEASURE AND ANALYZE TRENDS IN HAZARDOUS WASTE RECYCLING.CONDUCT STUDIES TO DETERMINE THE SAFETY OF SPECIFIC USES OF INDUSTRIAL MATERIALS OR CHEMICALS WHEN RECYCLED.IDENTIFY CROSS-CUTTING ISSUES OF NATIONAL SIGNIFICANCE AFFECTING THE PCB DISPOSAL APPROVAL PROGRAM AND ISSUE GUIDANCE TO CLARIFY SUCH ISSUES.HEADQUARTERS AND REGIONSPROVIDE HAZARDOUS WASTE MANAGEMENT DATA TO SUPPORT RCRA INFO, THE NATIONAL REPORTING SYSTEM.PROVIDE ASSISTANCE IN WASTE CHARACTERIZATION AND DISPOSAL IN HOMELAND SECURITY INCIDENTS (E.G., NATURAL DISASTERS, OTHER EVENTS).DEVELOP OUTREACH AND TRAINING MATERIALS TO SUPPORT HAZARDOUS WASTE RECYCLING ACTIVITIES, PARTICULARLY BY SMALL BUSINESSES.CONDUCT NATIONAL MEETINGS TO EXCHANGE INFORMATION WITH INDUSTRY AND THE STATES ON HAZARDOUS WASTE RECYCLING EFFORTS.DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT SPECIAL PARTNERSHIPS WITH INDUSTRY, STATE, TRIBAL, AND LOCAL GOVERNMENTS, AND OTHER ORGANIZATIONS TO FACILITATE INCREASED HAZARDOUS WASTE RECYCLING AND DEMONSTRATE THE SAFE USE OF INDUSTRIAL MATERIALS.DEVELOP, STRENGTHEN, AND IMPLEMENT PROJECTS AND ACTIVITIES, SUCH AS EPA?S WASTEWISE PROGRAM, INVOLVING SUSTAINABLE MATERIALS MANAGEMENT (SMM).REVIEW SITE-SPECIFIC PETITIONS AND VARIANCES TO SUPPORT ENVIRONMENTALLY SOUND HAZARDOUS WASTE RECYCLING ACTIVITIES. DEVELOP INFORMATIONAL OUTREACH PRODUCTS THAT PROMOTE THE SAFE USE OF INDUSTRIAL MATERIALS AND CHEMICALS.PREPARE AND ISSUE PCB DISPOSAL APPROVALS, AND RESPOND TO INQUIRIES FROM STATES AND THE REGULATED COMMUNITY.REGIONS ONLYWORK WITH STATES TO ISSUE PERMITS TO ADDRESS ENVIRONMENTAL RISK AT ACTIVE HAZARDOUS WASTE MANAGEMENT FACILITIES AND RECYCLING FACILITIES AND TO PUT APPROVED CONTROLS IN PLACE.ISSUE POST-CLOSURE PERMITS OR USE APPROPRIATE ENFORCEMENT MECHANISMS TO ADDRESS ENVIRONMENTAL RISK AT INACTIVE LAND DISPOSAL FACILITIES AND TO PUT APPROVED CONTROLS IN PLACE. APPROVE CLOSURE PLANS FOR INTERIM STATUS TREATMENT AND STORAGE FACILITIES THAT ARE NOT SEEKING PERMITS TO OPERATE.PROVIDE TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE, EDUCATION, AND OUTREACH FOR ONGOING RCRA PROGRAMS SUCH AS MUNICIPAL SOLID WASTE (MSW) LANDFILLS, COMBUSTION, AND TRIBAL ISSUES.ENSURE THE STATES/TRIBES HAVE THE CAPACITY TO ADDRESS THE PERMITTING OF HAZARDOUS WASTE RECYCLING FACILITIES.FUNDS PROVIDED UNDER THIS PROGRAM/PROJECT MAY BE UTILIZED TO OBTAIN SEE PROGRAM RESOURCES TO SUPPORT EPA HEADQUARTERS AND/OR REGIONAL OFFICES UNDER THE AUTHORITY OF THE "ENVIRONMENTAL PROGRAMS ASSISTANCE ACT" PUBLIC LAW 98-313, JUNE 12, 1984.
Definition: EPA WILL PREVENT DANGEROUS RELEASES TO THE ENVIRONMENT FROM RCRA HAZARDOUS WASTE GENERATORS, TRANSPORTERS, AND TREATMENT, STORAGE, AND DISPOSAL (TSD) FACILITIES. EPA WILL REDUCE MATERIALS AND ENERGY USE THROUGH PRODUCT AND PROCESS REDESIGN AND INCREASE MATERIALS AND ENERGY RECOVERY FROM WASTES OTHERWISE REQUIRING DISPOSAL.

Name: RCRA:  WASTE MINIMIZATION & RECYCLING
Title: A2 - RCRA:  WASTE MINIMIZATION & RECYCLING
Laws: SOLID WASTE DISPOSAL ACT (1965), AS AMENDED BY THE RESOURCE CONSERVATION AND RECOVERY ACT (1976), 42UNITED STATES CODE 6901 ET SEQ. ? SECTIONS 1002, 1003, 2002 AND 8001.
Narrative: THE RESOURCE CONSERVATION AND RECOVERY ACT (RCRA) CALLS FOR NATIONAL LEADERSHIP TO REDUCE THE AMOUNT OF WASTE GENERATED AND TO IMPROVE THE RECOVERY AND CONSERVATION OF MATERIALS. SUSTAINABLE MATERIALS MANAGEMENT (SMM) TAKES A LIFE-CYCLE APPROACH TO MATERIALS USE AND WASTE PREVENTION BY EXAMINING THE EFFICIENCIES OF THE MATERIAL STREAM FROM THE POINT OF RESOURCE EXTRACTION THROUGH FINAL WASTE MANAGEMENT AND DISPOSAL.  THE IMPLEMENTATION OF SUSTAINABLE MATERIALS MANAGEMENT (SMM) IS FUNDAMENTAL TO ENSURING THAT ADEQUATE NATURAL RESOURCES (I.E., MATERIALS AND ENERGY) ARE AVAILABLE TO MEET TODAY?S NEEDS AND THOSE OF THE FUTURE.  IN FY 2012, EPA, THROUGH THE RCRA PROGRAM, WILL FOCUS ON THE ADVANCEMENT OF SMM, AND SPECIFICALLY:1.PROVIDE NATIONAL LEADERSHIP AND DIRECTION ON SUSTAINABLE MATERIALS MANAGEMENT,2.ENSURE THE SAFE AND EFFECTIVE REUSE/RECYCLING OF MATERIALS,3.CONVENE PARTIES WHO WOULD OTHERWISE NOT COME TOGETHER?INDUSTRY, STATE/LOCAL GOVERNMENT REPRESENTATIVES, NON-GOVERNMENTAL ORGANIZATIONS, AND OTHER STAKEHOLDERS?TO PURSUE SOLUTIONS TO RESOURCE CONSERVATION AND RECOVERY,4.DEVELOP AND PROMOTE NATIONAL SOLUTIONS FOR WASTE MANAGEMENT, RECYCLING, AND REUSE,5.ENCOURAGE INDUSTRY TO PURSUE INNOVATIVE POLICIES AND SOLUTIONS TO NON-REGULATED ENVIRONMENTAL PROBLEMS, AND TO EXCEED MINIMUM COMPLIANCE LEVELS FOR REGULATED ENVIRONMENTAL PROBLEMS, AND6.PROVIDE CREDIBLE SCIENTIFIC INFORMATION AND DATA.HEADQUARTERS AND REGIONAL OFFICE EFFORTS IN SMM RELY ON PARTNERSHIPS WITH STATE, TRIBAL, AND LOCAL GOVERNMENTS; BUSINESS, ACADEMIA AND INDUSTRY; AND NON-GOVERNMENTAL ORGANIZATIONS.  THE AGENCY IMPLEMENTS PROGRAMS WHICH: (A)  FOSTER SMM IN BUSINESS, INDUSTRY, NGOS, AND STATE AND LOCAL GOVERNMENTS; (B) ENCOURAGE LOCAL ADOPTION OF ECONOMIC INCENTIVES THAT FURTHER SMM; (C) PROMOTE INDUSTRIES IMPLEMENTING SUSTAINABLE PRACTICES RESULTING IN JOB GROWTH; (D) FOSTER IMPROVED SMM  BY COMMUNITIES AND TRIBES; (E) INCREASE PROCUREMENT OF GOODS MANUFACTURED WITH THE MOST EFFICIENT AND SUSTAINABLE USE OF RESOURCES AND MINIMIZED WASTE AT END OF LIFE; (F) ENCOURAGE INNOVATIVE PRACTICES THAT RESULT IN MORE COST-EFFECTIVE, SAFE SOURCE REDUCTION AND RECYCLING, INCLUDING THE SAFE RE-USE AND RECYCLING OF INDUSTRIAL MATERIALS; AND (G) PROVIDE INFORMATION TO ASSESS AND TRACK PROGRESS IN MEETING THESE TARGETS.  WHILE THESE PROGRAMS PROVIDE IMPORTANT NATIONAL LEADERSHIP AND SUPPORT FOR SMM, ACHIEVEMENT OF THESE OBJECTIVES REQUIRES INVESTMENT AND EFFORTS BY OTHER GOVERNMENTS, BUSINESS, VOLUNTARY ORGANIZATIONS, AND INDIVIDUALS. EXAMPLES OF MAJOR ACTIVITY AREAS IN WHICH EPA HEADQUARTERS AND REGIONAL OFFICES ARE ENGAGED INCLUDE:DEVELOPING AND USING COMPREHENSIVE PROCUREMENT GUIDELINES BASED ON MULTI-ATTRIBUTE CHARACTERISTICS TO PROMOTE GOVERNMENT-WIDE PROCUREMENT OF SUSTAINABLE PRODUCTS.WORKING WITH EXTERNAL STAKEHOLDERS AND OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES TO ENSURE THE SAFE MANAGEMENT OF USED ELECTRONICS.IMPLEMENTING STRATEGIES TO INCREASE FOOD WASTE PREVENTION WHICH INCLUDE WASTE REDUCTION, RECYCLING/COMPOSTING, AND REUSE. STRENGTHENING EPA?S SUCCESSFUL WASTEWI$E PROGRAM TO SUPPORT ADVANCEMENT OF SMM.DEVELOPING TOOLS AND METHODS TO MEASURE SMM?S ENVIRONMENTAL RESULTS. DEVELOPING AND SUPPORTING OUTREACH AND EDUCATION ON RESOURCE CONSERVATION THROUGH SMM.FUNDS PROVIDED UNDER THIS PROGRAM/PROJECT MAY BE UTILIZED TO OBTAIN SEE PROGRAM RESOURCES TO SUPPORT EPA HEADQUARTERS AND/OR REGIONAL OFFICES UNDER THE AUTHORITY OF THE "ENVIRONMENTAL PROGRAMS ASSISTANCE ACT" PUBLIC LAW 98-313, JUNE 12, 1984.
Definition: EPA WILL REDUCE MATERIALS AND ENERGY USE THROUGH PRODUCT AND PROCESS REDESIGN AND INCREASE MATERIALS AND ENERGY RECOVERY FROM WASTES OTHERWISE REQUIRING DISPOSAL.  EPA WILL ALSO REDUCE POLLUTION IN BUSINESS THROUGH ADOPTION OF MORE EFFICIENT, SUSTAINABLE AND PROTECTIVE POLICIES, PRACTICES, MATERIALS AND TECHNOLOGIES.

Name: INTEGRATED ENVIRONMENTAL STRATEGIES
Title: A4 - INTEGRATED ENVIRONMENTAL STRATEGIES
Laws: CWA, SECTION 104(B)(3); CAA, SECTION 104(B)(3).
Narrative: PROMOTING SMART GROWTH AND SUSTAINABLE DESIGN SINCE 1996, EPA'S SMART GROWTH AND SUSTAINABLE DESIGN WORK HAS HELPED COMMUNITY AND GOVERNMENT LEADERS IMPROVE THE ENVIRONMENTAL OUTCOMES OF THEIR DEVELOPMENT DECISIONS. ACTIVITIES INCLUDE:? CONDUCTING RESEARCH AND DEVELOPING TOOLS THAT HELP COMMUNITIES SEE THE CONNECTION BETWEEN DEVELOPMENT AND THE ENVIRONMENT, THE ECONOMY, AND PUBLIC HEALTH. ? PROVIDING TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO STATES, REGIONS, AND LOCAL AND TRIBAL GOVERNMENTS. ? ENGAGING AND LEVERAGING ACTIVITIES WITH OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES.CONDUCTING RESEARCH AND DEVELOPING TOOLS. EPA?S RESEARCH ON EMERGING TRENDS SERVES AS THE FOUNDATION FOR DEVELOPING TOOLS THAT WILL BE USEFUL TO COMMUNITIES AND ALL LEVELS OF GOVERNMENT. FOR EXAMPLE, EPA RESEARCHED THE WATER QUALITY BENEFITS OF SMART GROWTH STRATEGIES SUCH AS REDEVELOPMENT AND HIGH DENSITY. THIS RESEARCH LED TO A NUMBER OF RESOURCES FOR STATE AND LOCAL GOVERNMENTS, INCLUDING A WATER QUALITY SCORECARD TO HELP COMMUNITIES PROTECT WATER RESOURCES AND SUPPORT ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT. THIS SCORECARD IS NOW THE BASIS FOR TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO LOCAL GOVERNMENTS SEEKING TO ALIGN THEIR WATER INFRASTRUCTURE INVESTMENTS WITH THEIR COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT GOALS. EPA WILL: ? DEVELOP TOOLS AND INFORMATION ON OPTIONS FOR EQUITABLE DEVELOPMENT FOR DISADVANTAGED COMMUNITIES. ? PROVIDE A RANGE OF PERFORMANCE MEASURES AND TOOLS TO HELP RURAL, SUBURBAN, AND URBAN COMMUNITIES ASSESS THEIR SMART GROWTH APPROACHES. ? DEVELOP TOOLS AND POLICY OPTIONS TO HELP COMMUNITIES REDUCE THEIR ENERGY USE AND CARBON EMISSIONS THROUGH BETTER DEVELOPMENT AND LOCATION DECISIONS. PROVIDING TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE. EPA PROVIDES DIRECT TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO STATE AND LOCAL GOVERNMENTS TO HELP THEM GROW THEIR ECONOMIES AND CREATE JOBS WHILE REDUCING POLLUTION. THERE IS TREMENDOUS DEMAND FOR THIS WORK. SINCE 2005, EPA HAS RECEIVED MORE THAN 1,100 TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE APPLICATIONS REPRESENTING OVER $43M IN REQUESTED FUNDING. DURING THIS SAME TIME PERIOD, EPA HAS ASSISTED OVER 150 COMMUNITIES. THIS WORK IS THE CORNERSTONE OF EPA?S SMART GROWTH APPROACH TO DEVELOPMENT-RELATED CHALLENGES IN COMMUNITIES. ENGAGING FEDERAL PARTNERS. EPA WILL CONTINUE TO PARTNER WITH OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES TO MEET THE GROWING DEMAND FROM COMMUNITIES FOR DIRECT TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE. FOR EXAMPLE, IN JUNE 2009, EPA, THE U.S. DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION, AND THE U.S. DEPARTMENT OF HOUSING AND URBAN DEVELOPMENT FORMED THE PARTNERSHIP FOR SUSTAINABLE COMMUNITIES TO HELP PROTECT THE ENVIRONMENT BY PROVIDING COMMUNITIES WITH MORE OPTIONS FOR PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION AND BETTER ACCESS TO GREEN, AFFORDABLE HOUSING. EPA WILL WORK WITH OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES TO STRENGTHEN COORDINATION AND ENSURE EFFICIENT USE OF FUNDS. EPA WILL ALSO WORK TO MAKE OUR RESOURCES AND THOSE FROM OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES EASIER FOR COMMUNITIES TO UNDERSTAND AND ACCESS. EPA WILL WORK WITH THE FEDERAL EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT AGENCY TO DEVELOP GUIDELINES AND PROCEDURES TO HELP COMMUNITIES PREPARE FOR DISASTERS AND REBUILD MORE SUSTAINABLY AFTER A DISASTER. EPA WILL CONTINUE TO PROVIDE SUPPORT TO OTHER FEDERAL AGENCIES, SUCH AS THE U.S. DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE, ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT ADMINISTRATION, AND THE NATIONAL OCEANIC AND ATMOSPHERIC ADMINISTRATION FOR ACTIVITIES INCLUDING JOINTLY DELIVERING TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO RURAL APPALACHIAN COMMUNITIES AND PROPOSING SUSTAINABILITY LANGUAGE TO INCLUDE IN GRANT SOLICITATIONS AND OTHER GUIDANCE DOCUMENTS. THIS ASSISTANCE HELPS THESE AGENCIES PROTECT THE ENVIRONMENT THROUGH THEIR COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT PROGRAMS, POLICIES, REGULATIONS, AND RESOURCES, WHILE MEETING THEIR CORE AGENCY OBJECTIVES. STRATEGIC ENVIRONMENTAL MANAGEMENT STRATEGIC ENVIRONMENTAL MANAGEMENT ADDRESSES OVERARCHING MANAGEMENT ISSUES ACROSS PROGRAMS AND REGIONS TO MAXIMIZE AGENCY EFFICIENCY AND EFFECTIVENESS. THESE ACTIVITIES INCLUDE PROGRAM ANALYSIS, COORDINATION AND DECISION-MAKING SUPPORT TO ENSURE THE EFFECTIVENESS AND EFFICIENCY OF AGENCY MANAGEMENT POLICIES AND PRACTICES; RIGOROUS PROGRAM EVALUATION TO IMPROVE PROGRAM DESIGN AND OUTCOMES; AND THE ANALYSIS AND MANAGEMENT OF EMERGING CROSS-CUTTING ENVIRONMENTAL POLICY ISSUES. PROGRAM ANALYSIS CONSIDERS MEASUREMENT INFORMATION AND OTHER DATA TO INFORM SENIOR LEVEL DECISION-MAKING ON MANAGEMENT AND OTHER ISSUES. DECISION-MAKING IS CONDUCTED THROUGH A SERIES OF REGULARLY SCHEDULED MEETINGS OF AGENCY LEADERSHIP WHICH EXAMINE HOW RELEVANT ORGANIZATIONS, PROGRAM ACTIVITIES, REGULATIONS, POLICIES, AND PRACTICES ARE CONTRIBUTING AS PLANNED TO AGENCY PRIORITIES. PROGRESS TOWARD AGENCY STRATEGIC GOALS IS ALSO ADVANCED THROUGH THE REGULAR REVIEW OF KEY METRICS. BUSINESS PROCESS IMPROVEMENT TECHNIQUES AND OTHER STRATEGIC MANAGEMENT PRACTICES FOR ENHANCING OPERATIONAL EFFECTIVENESS AND EFFICIENCY ARE ALSO IDENTIFIED AND DEPLOYED TO CONTINUALLY IMPROVE AGENCY PROGRAMS AND OPERATIONS. PERIODIC SYSTEMATIC ASSESSMENTS IN THE FORM OF PROGRAM EVALUATION STUDIES ARE USED TO GATHER EMPIRICAL EVIDENCE OF PROGRAM EFFECTIVENESS AND EFFICIENCY. PROGRAM EVALUATION STUDIES OBJECTIVELY ASSESS AGENCY PROGRAMS? EFFICIENCY AND COST-EFFECTIVENESS, ASSESS PROGRAMS? ABILITY TO ATTAIN OUTCOMES AND OBJECTIVES AND IDENTIFY ALTERNATIVE APPROACHES FOR EFFECTIVE PROGRAM STREAMLINING AND TO IMPROVE RESULTS. EPA WILL USE RIGOROUS EVALUATION METHODS; CONTRACT INDEPENDENT, OBJECTIVE THIRD-PARTY EVALUATORS TO CONDUCT STUDIES AS APPROPRIATE; ENSURE TRANSPARENCY OF EVALUATION STUDIES; AND ENSURE THAT DATA ARE MADE AVAILABLE TO EXTERNAL EVALUATORS TO ASSESS PROGRAMS. ANOTHER ASPECT OF STRATEGIC ENVIRONMENTAL MANAGEMENT INVOLVES ADDRESSING EMERGING, CROSS-CUTTING POLICY ISSUES TO ENSURE THAT THESE ISSUES RECEIVE PROMPT AND APPROPRIATE ATTENTION BY THE AGENCY. THIS MAY ALSO INVOLVE CONDUCTING ANALYSIS TO ENSURE UNDERSTANDING OF THE ISSUES AND DEVELOP POTENTIAL STRATEGIES FOR AGENCY ACTION ON THESE ISSUES. THE AGENCY WILL FOCUS ON PRIORITY ISSUES THAT ADVANCE ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION, COMPETITIVENESS, AND ECONOMIC GROWTH. EPA INTENDS TO FOCUS ADDITIONAL RESOURCES ON ITS BUSINESS PROCESS IMPROVEMENT (E.G., LEAN GOVERNMENT) EFFORTS TO REALIZE IMPORTANT COST, TIME AND/OR EFFICIENCY SAVINGS IN THREE AGENCY PROCESSES. CANDIDATE PROCESSES WILL BE CAREFULLY CHOSEN TO INCREASE THE LIKELIHOOD OF REALIZED TIME SAVINGS, PROCESS STREAMLINING, AND ULTIMATELY ENHANCED ENVIRONMENTAL PROGRAM EFFECTIVENESS. THIS DEDICATION OF RESOURCES TO STREAMLINING APPROACHES IS INTENDED TO INCREASE CAPACITY TO FOCUS EPA?S LIMITED RESOURCES ON THE MOST MISSION CRITICAL ACTIVITIES.
Definition: THE INTEGRATED ENVIRONMENTAL STRATEGIES PROGRAM SUPPORTS THE AGENCY?S SMART GROWTH AND SUSTAINABLE DESIGN WORK, AS WELL AS STRATEGIC ENVIRONMENTAL MANAGEMENT ACTIVITIES THAT ADDRESS CROSS-CUTTING AND EMERGING POLICY ISSUES. THIS PROGRAM PROVIDES TOOLS AND RESOURCES TO HELP COMMUNITIES BECOME MORE ENVIRONMENTALLY AND ECONOMICALLY RESILIENT, DEVELOPS STRATEGIES TO HELP BUSINESSES ADVANCE THEIR ENVIRONMENTAL AND ECONOMIC GOALS, AND PROMOTES EFFECTIVE MANAGEMENT POLICIES AND PRACTICES WITHIN THE AGENCY. THIS WORK IS IN HIGH DEMAND BY UNIVERSITIES, CITIZEN GROUPS, THE BUSINESS COMMUNITY, AND ALL LEVELS OF GOVERNMENT BECAUSE OF THE GREAT PROMISE IT HOLDS TO PRODUCE LASTING ECONOMIC, ENVIRONMENTAL, AND PUBLIC HEALTH BENEFITS.

Name: SCIENCE POLICY AND BIOTECHNOLOGY
Title: C2 - SCIENCE POLICY AND BIOTECHNOLOGY
Laws: FEDERAL ADVISORY COMMITTEE ACT (FACA)FEDERAL FUNGICIDE, INSECTICIDE & RODENTICIDE ACT (FIFRA) FEDERAL FOOD, DRUG & COSMETIC ACT (FFDCA) FOOD QUALITY PROTECTION ACT (FQPA) OF 1996 TOXIC SUBSTANCES CONTROL ACT (TSCA)
Narrative: ORGANIZE AND CONDUCT MAJOR PUBLICLY TRANSPARENT REVIEWS (TYPICALLY SIX TO TEN EACH YEAR) BY INDEPENDENT, OUTSIDE SCIENTIFIC EXPERTS OF KEY SCIENCE DOCUMENTS, SCIENCE POLICIES, AND/OR SCIENCE PROGRAMS THAT ARE CRITICAL TO EFFECTIVE EPA?S PESTICIDE PROGRAM ACTIVITIES.BIOTECHNOLOGY IS ILLUSTRATIVE OF THE WORK ENCOMPASSED BY THIS PROGRAM. MANY OFFICES WITHIN EPA REGULARLY DEAL WITH BIOTECHNOLOGY ISSUES, AND THE COORDINATION AMONG AFFECTED OFFICES ALLOWS FOR COHERENT AND CONSISTENT SCIENTIFIC POLICY FROM A BROAD AGENCY PERSPECTIVE.  THE BIOTECHNOLOGY TEAM ASSISTS IN FORMULATING EPA AND UNITED STATES POSITIONS ON BIOTECHNOLOGY ISSUES, INCLUDING REPRESENTATION ON UNITED STATES DELEGATIONS TO INTERNATIONAL MEETINGS WHEN NEEDEDDEVELOP AND/OR COORDINATE REVIEW OF MAJOR SCIENCE-BASED POLICIES AND RULES FOR ENVIRONMENTAL AND HUMAN HEALTH RISK EVALUATION OF GENETICALLY-MODIFIED ORGANISMS.
Definition: SCIENTIFIC ADVISORY PANEL (SAP), BIOTECHNOLOGY RULES, AND OTHER SCIENCE POLICY ISSUES.

Name: STATE AND LOCAL PREVENTION AND PREPAREDNESS
Title: C3 - STATE AND LOCAL PREVENTION AND PREPAREDNESS
Laws: EMERGENCY PLANNING AND COMMUNITY RIGHT-TO-KNOW ACT (EPCRA, 1986), 42 U.S.C. 11011 ET SEQ. ? SECTIONS 11011 ? 11023 AND THE CLEAN WATER ACT (1972), AS AMENDED BY THE CHEMICAL SAFETY INFORMATION, SITE SECURITY, AND FUELS REGULATORY RELIEF ACT (1999), 42 U.S.C. 7401 ET SEQ. ? SECTION 112(R).
Narrative: EFFORTS UNDER THIS PROGRAM ARE DESIGNED TO REDUCE THE RISK OF DEATH, INJURY, AND ENVIRONMENTAL DAMAGES THAT CAN RESULT FROM ACCIDENTAL CHEMICALS RELEASES AT INDUSTRIAL FACILITIES.  TO ACCOMPLISH THIS, ACTIVITIES ARE PRIMARILY FOCUSED ON GUIDANCE AND ASSISTANCE TO STATES, TRIBES AND LOCAL COMMUNITIES TO IMPLEMENT CHEMICAL ACCIDENT PREPAREDNESS AND PREVENTION PROGRAMS AND SERVE AS IMPLEMENTING AGENCY FOR STATES THAT ARE NOT RUNNING THEIR OWN PROGRAMS. ACTIVITIES INCLUDE CONDUCTING INSPECTIONS AT RISK MANAGEMENT PLAN (RMP) AND EPCRA FACILITIES TO ENSURE COMPLIANCE WITH REGULATIONS; WORK WITH COMMUNITIES TO PROVIDE CHEMICAL RISK INFORMATION ABOUT LOCAL FACILITIES; SUPPORT PLANNING AND RESPONSE TOOLS SUCH AS THE COMPUTER AIDED MANAGEMENT OF EMERGENCY OPERATIONS (CAMEO).PROVIDING NATIONAL COORDINATION FOR CHEMICAL ACCIDENT PREVENTION AND EMERGENCY RESPONSE PLANNING PROGRAM POLICY, INSPECTIONS, COMPLIANCE AND ENFORCEMENT.MAINTAIN THE RMP DATABASE AND CONDUCT TRAINING FOR EPA AND STATE IMPLEMENTING AGENCY RMP AND EPCRA INSPECTORS.FUNDS PROVIDED UNDER THIS PROGRAM/PROJECT MAY BE UTILIZED TO OBTAIN SEE PROGRAM RESOURCES TO SUPPORT EPA HEADQUARTERS AND/OR REGIONAL OFFICES UNDER THE AUTHORITY OF THE "ENVIRONMENTAL PROGRAMS ASSISTANCE ACT" PUBLIC LAW 98-313, JUNE 12, 1984.
Definition: EPA'S PROGRAM SUPPORTS THE STATE EMERGENCY RESPONSE COMMISSION (SERC) AND LOCAL EMERGENCY PLANNING COMMITTEE (LEPC) STRUCTURE THROUGH TOOLS, REGULATIONS AND GUIDANCE.  PROVIDE TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO THESE ENTITIES TO ASSESS CHEMICAL RISKS, DEVELOP AND REVISE EMERGENCY RESPONSE PLANS AND MAKE CHEMICAL INFORMATION AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC.

Name: STRATOSPHERIC OZONE: DOMESTIC PROGRAMS
Title: C4 - STRATOSPHERIC OZONE: DOMESTIC PROGRAMS
Laws: CLEAN AIR ACT AMENDMENTS OF 1990 (CAA), TITLE VI (42 U.S.C. 7671- 7671Q), AS FURTHER AMENDED BY SECTION 764 OF THE 1999 OMNIBUS CONSOLIDATED AND EMERGENCY SUPPLEMENTAL APPROPRIATIONS ACT (P.L. 105-277, OCTOBER 21, 1998)THE MONTREAL PROTOCOL ON SUBSTANCES THAT DEPLETE THE OZONE LAYER
Narrative: THIS PROGRAM/PROJECT CAN USE FUNDS TO OBTAIN SEE PROGRAM RESOURCES.  EPM - HQIMPLEMENT AND MANAGE PROGRAMS DESIGNED TO PHASE OUT OZONE DEPLETING CHEMICALS IN THE UNITED STATES.IMPLEMENT LIMITATIONS ON THE OZONE DEPLETING CHEMICALS HYDROCHLOROFLUOROCARBONS (HCFCS) AND METHYL BROMIDE.IDENTIFY AND DISSEMINATE INFORMATION RELATED TO SAFE ALTERNATIVES TO COMPOUNDS BEING PHASED-OUT.IMPLEMENT AND MANAGE RECYCLING PROGRAMS IN THE U.S.DEVELOP ENVIRONMENTAL DATA AND INFORM THE PUBLIC ABOUT THE HEALTH RISKS OF OVEREXPOSURE TO UV RADIATION.MANAGE ACTIVITIES RELATED TO ASSESSMENTS AND NEGOTIATIONS.EPM - RTIDENTIFY AND DISSEMINATE INFORMATION RELATED TO SAFE ALTERNATIVES TO COMPOUNDS BEING PHASED-OUT.DEVELOP ENVIRONMENTAL DATA AND INFORM THE PUBLIC ABOUT THE HEALTH RISKS OF OVEREXPOSURE TO UV RADIATION.PROVIDE POLICY AND PROGRAM INPUT TO THE OFFICE OF AIR AND RADIATION.
Definition: THIS PROGRAM INCLUDES ACTIVITIES FOR REGULATORY PROGRAMS TO RESTORE THE OZONE LAYER.

Name: STRATOSPHERIC OZONE: MULTILATERAL FUND
Title: C5 - STRATOSPHERIC OZONE: MULTILATERAL FUND
Laws: CLEAN AIR ACT AMENDMENTS OF 1990 (CAA), TITLE VI (42 U.S.C. 7671- 7671Q), AS FURTHER AMENDED BY SECTION 764 OF THE 1999 OMNIBUS CONSOLIDATED AND EMERGENCY SUPPLEMENTAL APPROPRIATIONS ACT  (P.L. 105-277, OCTOBER 21, 1998)THE MONTREAL PROTOCOL ON SUBSTANCES THAT DEPLETE THE OZONE LAYER
Narrative: ONLY EXTRAMURAL RESOURCES FOR THE MULTILATERAL FUND AUTHORIZED UNDER THE MONTREAL PROTOCOL CAN BE CHARGED TO THIS PROGRAM PROJECT.
Definition: THIS PROGRAM INCLUDES THE MULTILATERAL FUND THAT PROMOTES INTERNATIONAL COMPLIANCE WITH THE MONTREAL PROTOCOL BY FINANCING THE INCREMENTAL COST OF CONVERTING EXISTING INDUSTRIES IN DEVELOPING COUNTRIES TO COST-EFFECTIVE OZONE FRIENDLY TECHNOLOGY.

Name: SUPERFUND:  EMERGENCY RESPONSE AND REMOVAL
Title: C6 - SUPERFUND:  EMERGENCY RESPONSE AND REMOVAL
Laws: COMPREHENSIVE ENVIRONMENTAL RESPONSE, COMPENSATION AND LIABILITY ACT (1980), AS AMENDED, 42 UNITED STATES CODE SC 9601 ET SEQ. ? SECTIONS 104, 105, AND 106.
Narrative: PROGRAM ACTIVITIES INVOLVE TAKING ACTION WHEN LOCAL AND STATES RESOURCES ARE UNABLE TO PREVENT, REDUCE OR MITIGATE THREATS POSED BY RELEASES OR POTENTIAL RELEASES OF HAZARDOUS POLLUTANTS AND/OR OIL IN EMERGENCY AND NON-EMERGENCY SITUATIONS. THE PROGRAM PRIORITIZES AND PROVIDES SERVICES TO SITES KNOWN TO POSE THE GREATEST ACTUAL OR POTENTIAL RISK TO PUBLIC HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT.EPA REMOVAL RESOURCES ARE UTILIZED TO TRAIN, EQUIP, PROVIDE TECHNICAL SUPPORT IN ORDER TO MANAGE, CONTAIN AND REMOVE CONTAMINANTS THAT WILL, IF UNADDRESSED, HAVE A CRITICAL ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ON COMMUNITIES.  REMOVAL RESOURCES CAN BE UTILIZED AT EMERGENCY INCIDENTS, AS WELL AS TIME CRITICAL AND NON-TIME CRITICAL AND POTENTIALLY RESPONSIBLE PARTY (PRP) ACTIONS.EPA WILL SUPPORT THE UNITED STATES COAST GUARD NATIONAL RESPONSE CENTER (NRC), WHICH IS THE FEDERAL ENTRY POINT FOR REPORTING ALL OIL AND CHEMICAL DISCHARGES INTO THE ENVIRONMENT ANYWHERE IN THE UNITED STATES. FUNDS PROVIDED UNDER THIS PROGRAM/PROJECT MAY BE UTILIZED TO OBTAIN SEE PROGRAM RESOURCES TO SUPPORT EPA HEADQUARTERS AND/OR REGIONAL OFFICES UNDER THE AUTHORITY OF THE "ENVIRONMENTAL PROGRAMS ASSISTANCE ACT" PUBLIC LAW 98-313, JUNE 12, 1984.
Definition: ENSURE ALL RELEASES AND/OR POTENTIAL RELEASES OF HAZARDOUS SUBSTANCES ARE APPROPRIATELY ADDRESSED AT NPL AND NON-NPL RESPONSES FOR THE PROTECTION OF HUMAN HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT.  REMOVAL ON-SCENE COORDINATORS (OSCS) PROVIDE TECHNICAL SUPPORT AND ASSETS TO LOCAL, STATE, TRIBAL AND OTHER FEDERAL RESPONDERS.  CONDUCTS RESPONSES TO INCLUDING CHEMICAL, OIL, BIOLOGICAL, AND RADIOLOGICAL AGENTS. OSCS CAN DIRECT OR MONITOR RESPONSES BY RESPONSIBLE PARTIES OR OTHER AGENCIES.

Name: SUPERFUND:  ENFORCEMENT
Title: C7 - SUPERFUND:  ENFORCEMENT
Laws: COMPREHENSIVE ENVIRONMENTAL RESPONSE COMPENSATION, AND LIABILITY  ACT, AS AMENDED IN 1986 SMALL BUSINESS REGULATORY ENFORCEMENT FAIRNESS ACT OF 1996COMMUNITY ENVIRONMENTAL RESPONSE FACILITATION ACTNATIONAL ENVIRONMENTAL POLICY ACTATOMIC ENERGY ACTURANIUM MILL TAILINGS RADIATION LAND WITHDRAWAL ACTSAFE DRINKING WATER ACTCHROMINATED COOPER ARSENATEFEDERAL GRAND AND COOPERATIVE AGREEMENT ACTFEDERAL ACTIVITIES INVENTORY REFORM ACTFEDERAL ACQUISITION REGULATIONS, FEDERAL MANAGERS FINANCIALS INTEGRITY ACTFREEDOM OF INFORMATION ACTGOVERNMENT MANAGEMENT REFORM ACTIMPROPER PAYMENTS INFORMATION ACTINSPECTOR GENERAL ACTPAPERWORK REDUCTION ACTPRIVACY ACTCHIEF FINANCIAL OFFICERS ACTGOVERNMENT PERFORMANCE AND RESULTS ACTTHE PROMPT PAYMENT ACTEXECUTIVE ORDER 12241EXECUTIVE ORDER 12656
Narrative: THE SUPERFUND ENFORCEMENT PROGRAM/PROJECT DIRECTS ITS RESOURCES TO MAXIMIZE PRP INVOLVEMENT IN CONDUCTING OR FUNDING CLEANUPS AT CONTAMINATED SITES. THE PROGRAM ALSO IMPLEMENTS REFORMS AND OTHER EFFORTS TO INCREASE FAIRNESS, REDUCE TRANSACTION COSTS, AND ENCOURAGE ECONOMIC RE-DEVELOPMENT.  THE FOCUS IS ON GETTING RESPONSIBLE PARTIES TO FUND WORK EARLY IN THE PROCESS AND ON RECOVERING APPROPRIATED SUPERFUND EXPENDITURES TO ENSURE THAT POLLUTERS PAY THE COSTS ASSOCIATED WITH CLEANING UP SITES.  THE AGENCY PROVIDES FUNDING TO THE DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE (DOJ) THROUGH AN INTERAGENCY AGREEMENT (IA) TO ASSIST EPA IN ITS SUPERFUND ENFORCEMENT EFFORTS.CERCLA MANDATES CERTAIN ACTIVITIES, INCLUDING THE FOLLOWING: 1)  APPROPRIATE RESPONSES; 2) ISSUANCE OF ADMINISTRATIVE ORDERS AND INITIATION OF JUDICIAL ACTIONS TO COMPEL PRPS TO REMEDIATE SITES WHICH MAY POSE AN IMMINENT AND SUBSTANTIAL ENDANGERMENT TO PUBLIC HEALTH OR WELFARE OR THE ENVIRONMENT; 3) NEGOTIATE  SETTLEMENTS WITH PRPS FOR VOLUNTARY RESPONSE ACTIONS UNDER OVERSIGHT BY EPA; 4) JUDICATE OR ENTER INTO ADMINISTRATIVE SETTLEMENTS WITH PRPS FOR COST RECOVERY; 5)ENTER INTO AGREEMENTS WITH STATES TO ACT ON BEHALF OF THE FEDERAL GOVERNMENT;  6) ENFORCE VIOLATIONS OF REPORTING REQUIREMENTS; AND, 7) ENFORCE PRP COMPLIANCE WITH SETTLEMENTS AND ORDERS FOR SITE CLEANUP. COST DOCUMENTATION AND COST RECOVERY ACTIVITIES ARE PROVIDED BY REGIONAL AND HEADQUARTERS FINANCE OFFICES.  THESE EFFORTS INCLUDE TRACKING AND MANAGING SUPERFUND DELINQUENT DEBT, MAINTAINING THE SUPERFUND COST RECOVERY PACKAGE IMAGING AND ON-LINE SYSTEM (SCORPIOS), AND USING SCORPIOS TO PREPARE COST DOCUMENTATION PACKAGES. THE PROGRAM WILL CONTINUE TO REFINE AND STREAMLINE THE COST DOCUMENTATION PROCESS TO GAIN FURTHER EFFICIENCIES, PROVIDE THE DOJ CASE SUPPORT FOR SUPERFUND SITES, AND CALCULATE INDIRECT COST AND ANNUAL ALLOCATION RATES TO BE APPLIED TO DIRECT COSTS INCURRED BY THE EPA FOR SITE CLEANUP. THE PROGRAM ALSO WILL CONTINUE TO MAINTAIN THE ACCOUNTING AND BILLING OF SUPERFUND OVERSIGHT COSTS ATTRIBUTABLE TO RESPONSIBLE PARTIES. THESE COSTS REPRESENT THE EPA?S COST OF OVERSEEING SUPERFUND SITE CLEANUP EFFORTS BY RESPONSIBLE PARTIES AS STIPULATED IN THE TERMS OF SETTLEMENT AGREEMENTS. IN ADDITION, GRANTS MAY FUND SUPPORT FROM NON-PROFIT ORGANIZATIONS FOR SEE ENROLLEES TO WORK ON OCFO SUPERFUND COST RECOVERY ACTIVITIES.THE FOLLOWING IS A DESCRIPTION OF THE PROGRAM?S MANDATES AND A LIST OF ACTIVITIES WHICH ARE EXECUTED BY EPA HEADQUARTERS AND/ OR REGIONAL OFFICES UNDER THIS PROGRAM/PROJECT.HEADQUARTERS AND/OR REGIONSIDENTIFY PRPS AS EARLY AS POSSIBLE IN THE PROCESS TO MAXIMIZE THE NUMBER OF CLEANUPS CONDUCTED OR FUNDED BY RESPONSIBLE PARTIES.USE ENFORCEMENT TOOLS TO NEGOTIATE AND ACHIEVE SETTLEMENT FOR LONG-TERM REMEDIAL RESPONSE AND SHORT-TERM RESPONSE ACTIONS AT SUPERFUND SITES.ASSURE FAIRNESS IN SETTLEMENTS BY PURSUING DE MINIMIS AND DE MICROMIS SETTLEMENTS, ORPHAN SHARE DETERMINATIONS AND COMPENSATION, SETTLEMENTS WITH MUNICIPALITIES, AND WITH PARTIES WITH LIMITED ABILITY TO PAY; AND, ON USING SUCH TOOLS AS ALTERNATIVE DISPUTE RESOLUTION TO MINIMIZE TRANSACTION COSTS.MAKE FORMERLY CONTAMINATED PARCELS OF LAND AVAILABLE FOR RESIDENTIAL, COMMERCIAL AND INDUSTRIAL RE-USE BY ADDRESSING LIABILITY CONCERNS THROUGH THE ISSUANCE OF COMFORT LETTER AND PROSPECTIVE PURCHASER AGREEMENTS.MAINTAIN AN AGGRESSIVE COST RECOVERY PROGRAM IN CASES WHERE APPROPRIATED DOLLARS HAVE BEEN USED TO CLEANUP SITES AND PURSUE  CASES IN EXCESS OF $500,000 THAT ARE NEARING THEIR STATUTE OF LIMITATIONS. CONDUCT ACTIVITIES TO SUPPORT TIMELY BILLINGS AND COLLECTIONS.REGIONSENFORCE PRP COMPLIANCE WITH PERFORMANCE OR PAYMENT OBLIGATIONS FOR LONG-TERM REMEDIAL AND SHORT-TERM RESPONSE ACTIONS TO ENSURE TIMELY AND PROTECTIVE CLEANUP AT SUPERFUND SITES.
Definition: THE SUPERFUND ENFORCEMENT PROGRAM EMPHASIZES ?ENFORCEMENT FIRST? TO ENSURE THAT SITES FOR WHICH THERE ARE VIABLE RESPONSIBLE PARTIES ARE CLEANED UP BY THOSE PARTIES.  IN TANDEM WITH THIS APPROACH, VARIOUS SUPERFUND REFORMS ARE BEING IMPLEMENTED TO INCREASE FAIRNESS, REDUCE TRANSACTION COSTS, AND PROMOTE ECONOMIC REDEVELOPMENT.  EPA NEGOTIATES REMEDIAL DESIGN/REMEDIAL ACTION CLEAN-UP AGREEMENTS AND ACHIEVES REMOVAL AGREEMENTS AT HAZARDOUS WASTE SITES.  WHERE NEGOTIATIONS FAIL, THE AGENCY TAKES EITHER UNILATERAL ENFORCEMENT ACTIONS TO REQUIRE ?POTENTIALLY RESPONSIBLE PARTY? (PRP) CLEAN-UP OR USES SUPERFUND APPROPRIATED DOLLARS TO REMEDIATE SITES.  WHEN APPROPRIATED DOLLARS ARE USED TO REMEDIATE SITES, COST RECOVERY ACTIONS ARE TAKEN AGAINST PRP?S TO RECOUP EXPENDITURES.

Name: SUPERFUND:  EPA EMERGENCY PREPAREDNESS
Title: C8 - SUPERFUND:  EPA EMERGENCY PREPAREDNESS
Laws: COMPREHENSIVE ENVIRONMENTAL RESPONSE, COMPENSATION AND LIABILITY ACT (1980), AS AMENDED, 42 UNITED STATES CODE 9601 ET SEQ. ? SECTIONS 104, 105, AND 106; ROBERT T STAFFORD DISASTER RELIEF AND EMERGENCY ASSISTANCE ACT, (1974) AS AMENDED, 42 UNITED STATES CODE 5121 ET SEQ.
Narrative: THE PROGRAM IS THE AGENCY'S DESIGNATED LEAD FOR THE NATIONAL RESPONSE FRAMEWORK (NRF) OIL AND HAZARDOUS MATERIALS RESPONSE ANNEX - EMERGENCY FUNCTION #10 WHICH COVERS HAZARDOUS MATERIALS, OIL, AND OTHER CONTAMINANTS.  DEVELOP PLANS, PROCEDURES AND STANDARDS TO RESPOND TO RELEASES OF HAZARDOUS SUBSTANCES, OIL SPILLS, TERRORIST ATTACKS, MAJOR DISASTERS OR OTHER EMERGENCIES, WHETHER ACCIDENTAL OR INTENTIONAL. CHAIR THE NRT/RRTS AND PROVIDE POLICY AND MANAGEMENT SUPPORT.  ENSURE COORDINATION OF THE NRT/RRTS AND NRF ACTIVITIES.  PROVIDE ASSISTANCE IN DEVELOPING AND EXERCISING FEDERAL EMERGENCY PLANS (AND THE INTERACTION WITH STATE AND LOCAL PLANS).PLAN AND COORDINATES FEDERAL INTERAGENCY PREPAREDNESS AND PREVENTION ACTIVITIES.ASSIST IN DEVELOPING LOCAL HAZMAT TEAM CAPABILITIES, PROTECTIVE ACTION DECISION SYSTEMS AND OTHER PREPAREDNESS ACTIVITIES; COORDINATING WITH REGIONAL GOVERNMENT, INDUSTRY AND PUBLIC INTEREST GROUPS ON CERCLA ACTIVITIES.SUPPORT AND PARTICIPATE IN NATIONAL LEVEL EXERCISES TO CONTINUE A WORKING RELATIONSHIP WITH STATE, LOCAL AND FEDERAL RESPONDERS TRAINING IN EMERGENCY PLANNING, PREPAREDNESS, HAZARDS ANALYSIS AND RESPONSE.  ACTIVITIES ENSURE TECHNICAL EXPERTISE AND RESOURCES ARE BROUGHT TO BEAR TO HELP STATES DURING AND AFTER LARGE-SCALE RELEASES OF HAZARDOUS MATERIALS.FUNDS PROVIDED UNDER THIS PROGRAM/PROJECT MAY BE UTILIZED TO OBTAIN SEE PROGRAM RESOURCES TO SUPPORT EPA HEADQUARTERS AND/OR REGIONAL OFFICES UNDER THE AUTHORITY OF THE "ENVIRONMENTAL PROGRAMS ASSISTANCE ACT" PUBLIC LAW 98-313, JUNE 12, 1984.
Definition: ENSURE READINESS OF EPA PREPAREDNESS AND RESPONSE PERSONNEL THROUGH PLANNING, TRAINING, AND EXERCISES.
