DCIS; DATA REVIEWS; SCIENCE OR SCIENCE POLICY WORK, SUCH AS TOXICOLOGY, PHARMACOLOGICAL STUDY REVIEWS, RISK ASSESSMENT, REVIEW OF INERT DATA, FATE AND TRANSPORT STUDIES, USE AND USAGE INFORMATION), LAB SUPPORT,  PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT AND FINANCIAL, CONTRACT GRANT MANAGEMENT AND OVERSIGHT.RISK REDUCTION IMPLEMENTATION  (HQ AND REGIONS):  THE ?FRONT-LINE RISK MANAGEMENT? WORK IN THE PESTICIDE PROGRAM IN THE EPM ACCOUNT. -HQ: - INTERNATIONAL WORK THAT IS NOT DIRECTLY RELATED TO LICENSING WORK, ( I.E. WORK ON GENERAL TREATIES MAY BE INCLUDED HERE, BUT JOINT REVIEWS WOULD NOT BE INCLUDED HERE)-HQ AND REGIONS:  COMMUNICATION, OUTREACH OR EDUCATIONAL ACTIVITIES NOT DIRECTLY RELATED TO A LICENSING ACTIVITY SUCH AS PESTICIDE ENVIRONMENTAL STEWARDSHIP, STRATEGIC AGRICULTURAL INITIATIVE, INTEGRATED PEST MANAGEMENT, LAWNS AND ENVIRONMENT INITIATIVE, RELATED ENFORCEMENT CASE REVIEWS; SUPPORTING IT; PERFORMANCE MEASUREMENT AND FINANCIAL, CONTRACT, GRANT MANAGEMENT AND OVERSIGHT.RULEMAKING:  INCLUDES ONLY THOSE ACTIONS IN SUPPORT OF AN ADMINISTRATIVE PROCEDURE ACT (APA)-PROCESS RULEMAKING; REGULATORY SUPPORT SUCH AS DEVELOPMENT OF AN ECONOMIC ANALYSIS AND WORK GROUP PARTICIPATION.PROGRAM MANAGEMENT:  ADMINISTRATIVE/INFRASTRUCTURE NOT DIRECTLY RELATED TO LICENSING, ADMINISTERING GENERAL CONTRACTS (I.E. NOT SUPPORTING LICENSING ACTIVITIES), IT SEAT MANAGEMENT, AA STAFF  (AH 20).
Definition: REALIZING THE VALUE OF PESTICIDE AVAILABILITY WILL BE ACCOMPLISHED BY IMPLEMENTING OUR STATUTES AND TAKING REGULATORY ACTIONS TO ENSURE PUBLIC HEALTH AND SOCIO-ECONOMIC BENEFITS OF PESTICIDE AVAILABILITY AND USE ARE ACHIEVED. THE PESTICIDE ENVIRONMENTAL STEWARDSHIP PROGRAM?S (PESP) EFFORTS TO INCREASE ADOPTION OF INTEGRATED PEST MANAGEMENT (IPM) IN SCHOOLS HAS LED TO A SUBSTANTIAL REDUCTION IN PEST CONTROL COSTS AND A REDUCTION IN BOTH PESTICIDE APPLICATIONS AND PEST PROBLEMS IN PARTICIPATING SCHOOLS.LABORATORY ACTIVITIES FOR THE PESTICIDES PROGRAM SUPPORT THE GOAL OF PROTECTING THE ENVIRONMENT FROM PESTICIDE USE THROUGH THREE PESTICIDES LABORATORIES:  AN ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY LABORATORY, A MICROBIOLOGY LABORATORY AT THE ENVIRONMENTAL SCIENCE CENTER AT FORT MEADE, MD, AND AN ENVIRONMENTAL CHEMISTRY LABORATORY AT STENNIS SPACE CENTER, BAY ST. LOUIS, MS.

Name: INTERNATIONAL SOURCES OF POLLUTION
Title: J4 - INTERNATIONAL SOURCES OF POLLUTION
Laws: IN CONJUNCTION WITH NEPA SECTION 102(2)(F); : CAA 103(A), 42 U.S.C. 7403(A); CWA 104(A)(1) AND (2), 33 USC 1254(A)(1) AND (2); SDWA 1442(A)(1), 42 U.S.C. 300J-1(A)(1); SWDA 8001(A)(1), 42 U.S.C. 6981(A)(1);  FIFRA ?17(D) AND 20(A) , 7 U.S.C. ?136O(D) AND  136R(A); TSCA?10(A) OF THE TOXIC SUBSTANCES CONTROL ACT (TSCA), 15 U.S.C. ?2609(A) (IN CONSULTATION AND COOPERATION WITH THE DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH AND HUMAN SERVICES AND WITH OTHER APPROPRIATE DEPARTMENTS AND AGENCIES); MPRSA 203(A)(1), 33 U.S.C. 1443(A)(1), 42 U.S.C. 4332.
Narrative: INTERNATIONAL CAPACITY-BUILDING PLAYS A KEY ROLE IN PROTECTING HUMAN HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT BY PROVIDING TECHNICAL COOPERATION TO HELP COUNTRIES REDUCE AIR POLLUTION, BETTER MANAGE AIR QUALITY, AND REDUCE THE GLOBAL USE AND EMISSION OF MERCURY. THE DEPLETION OF NATURAL RESOURCES, SUCH AS FOREST COVER IN ONE NATION CAN HAVE ENVIRONMENTAL AND ECONOMIC CONSEQUENCES IN MANY OTHER COUNTRIES.AIR QUALITY IN THE U.S. IS AFFECTED BY EMISSIONS FROM OTHER COUNTRIES, SUCH AS PARTICLES, MERCURY AND TOXICS, WHICH CAN HAVE A DETRIMENTAL IMPACT ON HUMAN HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT IN THE U.S.  THE EPA WILL WORK TO TRANSFER APPROPRIATE AIR MANAGEMENT TOOLS AND TECHNIQUES TO KEY COUNTRIES AND REGIONS AS WE COLLABORATE WITH PARTNERS TO IMPROVE AIR QUALITY,  IN ADDITION, WE WILL CONTINUE EFFORTS TO REDUCE GLOBAL SOURCES OF PERSISTENT BIOACCUMULATIVE TOXINS CONTINUE TO BE A PRIORITY FOR THE EPA.  LONG-RANGE TRANSBOUNDARY ATMOSPHERIC TRANSPORT AND DEPOSITION  OF POLYCHLORINATED BIPHENYLS (PCBS), PESTICIDES, DIOXINS, AND FURANS CONTINUE TO THREATEN HUMAN HEALTH AND ECOSYSTEMS.  DOMESTIC POPS RELATED ACTIVITIES AND ASSOCIATED FUNDING ARE INCLUDED IN THE TOXIC SUBSTANCES: CHEMICAL RISK MANAGEMENT PROGRAM.  THE EPA WILL CONTINUE TO PROMOTE THE DEVELOPMENT OF ACTIVITIES FOR MANAGING DRINKING AND SURFACE WATER QUALITY ISSUES WORLDWIDE. EPA IS WORKING WITH NATIONAL GOVERNMENTS SUCH AS AFRICA, CHINA AND LATIN AMERICA TO BUILD REGULATORY FRAMEWORKS FOR WASTEWATER DISCHARGES TO ADDRESS WATER QUALITY ISSUES WORLDWIDE.  ALSO, THE EPA WILL CONTINUE TO PROVIDE TECHNICAL COOPERATION, EXPERTISE, AND ASSISTANCE TO HELP COMMUNITIES AND COUNTRIES TO PRESERVE AND RESTORE THE LAND AND TO MITIGATE SOURCES OF LAND POLLUTION.THE EPA WILL CONTINUE TO PROVIDE TECHNICAL COOPERATION, EXPERTISE, AND ASSISTANCE TO HELP COMMUNITIES AND COUNTRIES PRESERVE AND RESTORE THE LAND AND TO MITIGATE SOURCES OF LAND POLLUTION. THE EPA WILL HELP STRENGTHEN IMPLEMENTATION OF GLOBAL PROGRAMS TO ADDRESS ELECTRONIC WASTE (E-WASTE) AND SOUND REUSE AND RECYCLING OF ELECTRONIC EQUIPMENT.
Definition: THE EPA SAFEGUARDS AIR, WATER, AND LAND FOR ALL AMERICANS.  ADDRESSING ISSUES AT HOME IS ONLY PART OF THE EPA?S ENVIRONMENTAL EFFORT.  AS GLOBALIZATION INCREASES, WE GAIN AN UNDERSTANDING OF THE INTERDEPENDENCIES OF ECOSYSTEMS AND THE TRANSPORT OF POLLUTANTS FROM THEIR SOURCES.  IT BECOMES EVIDENT THAT THE ACTIONS OF OTHER COUNTRIES CAN AFFECT THE U.S. ENVIRONMENT.  IN MANY CASES, IT IS MORE EFFICIENT TO REDUCE EMISSIONS FROM FOREIGN SOURCES THAN FROM DOMESTIC ONES.  SOLVING THESE AND OTHER PROBLEMS REQUIRES STRONG COLLABORATION BETWEEN THE EPA AND ITS INTERNATIONAL PARTNERS.

Name: TRADE AND GOVERNANCE
Title: J5 - TRADE AND GOVERNANCE
Laws: IN CONJUNCTION WITH NEPA SECTION 102(2)(F); : CAA 103(A), 42 USC 7403(A); CWA 104(A)(1) AND (2), 33 USC 1254(A)(1) AND (2); SDWA 1442(A)(1), 42 USC 300J-1(A)(1); SWDA 8001(A)(1), 42 USC 6981(A)(1);  FIFRA ?17(D) AND 20(A) , 7 U.S.C. ?136O(D)AND  136R(A); TSCA?10(A) OF THE TOXIC SUBSTANCES CONTROL ACT (TSCA), 15 U.S.C. ?2609(A) (IN CONSULTATION AND COOPERATION WITH THE DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH AND HUMAN SERVICES AND WITH OTHER APPROPRIATE DEPARTMENTS AND AGENCIES); MPRSA 203(A)(1), 33 USC 1443(A)(1),  42 USC 4332; ANNUAL APPROPRIATION ACTS; EXECUTIVE ORDER 12915 (MAY 13, 1994) (IMPLEMENTATION OF NAFTA ENVIRONMENTAL SIDE AGREEMENT); EXECUTIVE ORDER 13141 (ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEW OF TRADE AGREEMENTS); EXECUTIVE ORDER 13277 (DELEGATION OF CERTAIN AUTHORITIES AND ASSIGNMENT OF CERTAIN FUNCTIONS UNDER THE TRADE ACT OF 2002), AS AMENDED BY E.O. 13346 (JULY 8, 2004)
Narrative: GOOD ENVIRONMENTAL GOVERNANCE ABROAD NOT ONLY YIELDS A CLEANER ENVIRONMENT, IT HELPS ENSURE THAT U.S. COMPANIES AND COMMUNITIES COMPETE ON AN EQUAL FOOTING IN THE INTERNATIONAL MARKETPLACE.  THE GROWING AWARENESS OF THE IMPACT OF TRADE ON THE ENVIRONMENT DEMONSTRATES THE NEED FOR EXPLICIT PRIORITIES AND OBJECTIVES FOR ENVIRONMENTAL ISSUES.  THROUGH LEADERSHIP IN THE COMMISSION ON ENVIRONMENTAL COOPERATION (CEC), THE ORGANIZATION FOR ECONOMIC COOPERATION AND DEVELOPMENT (OECD), AND OTHER INTERNATIONAL ENTITIES, EPA SUPPORTS ENVIRONMENTAL PERFORMANCE REVIEWS OF OTHER COUNTRIES SO THAT GOOD GOVERNANCE BEST PRACTICES  (SUCH AS PROVIDING ACCESS TO INFORMATION, COLLABORATING WITH DIVERSE STAKEHOLDERS, AND PROVIDING TRANSPARENCY IN ENVIRONMENTAL DECISION MAKING) ARE SHARED AND COUNTRIES CONTINUALLY IMPROVE.AN EXAMPLE OF PRIORITIES ARE TO CONTINUE PROVIDING INPUT TO U.S. ENGAGEMENT IN MULTILATERAL TRADE NEGOTIATIONS AND INITIATION AND/OR CONCLUSION OF NEW FREE TRADE AGREEMENTS, TRADE AND INVESTMENT FRAMEWORK AGREEMENTS, AND PROMOTING AND DEMONSTRATING ACTIVITIES TO MITIGATE ENVIRONMENTAL CONSEQUENCE DUE TO INCREASE TRADE.  EPA ALSO WILL PROVIDE TARGETED CAPACITY BUILDING SUPPORT UNDER THE ENVIRONMENTAL COOPERATION AGREEMENTS DEVELOPED PARALLEL TO U.S. FREE TRADE AGREEMENTS.  EPA ALSO WILL CONTINUE TO STRENGTHEN COOPERATION AND PROMOTE PUBLIC PARTICIPATION IN THE DEVELOPMENT AND IMPROVEMENT OF ENVIRONMENTAL LAWS, REGULATIONS, PROCEDURES, POLICIES AND PRACTICES.  EPA WILL SUPPORT THE CEC?S EFFORTS TO CATALYZE AND STRENGTHEN CAPACITY AND IMPROVE COMPLIANCE WITH ENVIRONMENTAL LAWS WHILE ENCOURAGING VOLUNTARY MEASURES ON THE PART OF INDUSTRY.  GRANTS ARE ISSUED TO GOVERNMENTAL AND NON-GOVERNMENTAL ORGANIZATIONS THAT SUPPORT THESE EFFORTS.
Definition: IN THE U.S., EPA PLAYS A KEY ROLE IN ENSURING TRADE RELATED ACTIVITIES SUSTAIN ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION.  GROWING U.S. GLOBAL TRADE UNDERSCORES THE IMPORTANCE OF ADDRESSING POTENTIAL ADVERSE ENVIRONMENTAL CONSEQUENCES.  INITIATIVES RELATING TO ENVIRONMENTAL TRADE AND INVESTMENT ARE CARRIED OUT BILATERALLY, MULTILATERALLY, OR IN CONNECTION WITH MULTILATERAL INSTITUTIONS SUCH AS THE WORLD TRADE ORGANIZATION  (WTO), WORLD BANK, EUROPEAN UNION (EU), OR ASIA-PACIFIC ECONOMIC COOPERATION FORUM (APEC).  ALSO, ACTIVITIES CARRIED OUT UNDER THIS  PROGRAM/PROJECT INCLUDE THOSE THAT SUPPORT PROTECTION OF HUMAN HEALTH AND ECOSYSTEM IN THE NORTH AMERICAN AGREEMENT ON ENVIRONMENTAL COOPERATION.

Name: LUST PREVENTION
Title: J6 - LUST PREVENTION
Laws: SOLID WASTE DISPOSAL ACT (SWDA) OF 1976, AS AMENDED; SUPERFUND REAUTHORIZATION AMENDMENTS OF 1986, SUBTITLE I; SECTION 9011 AND OTHER APPLICABLE PROVISIONS OF SUBTITLE I OF THE SWDA AS AMENDED FOR STATES AND TERRITORIES ENERGY POLICY ACT OF 2005; 42 U.S.C. 6916(F)(2); ENERGY POLICY ACT OF 2005, TITLE XV, ETHANOL AND MOTOR FUELS, SUBTITLE B, SECTIONS 1521 - 1533, PUBLIC LAW 109-58 (2005), 42 U.S.C. 15801; PUBLIC LAW 105-276 (1998); AND EPA'S ANNUAL APPROPRIATIONS ACT.
Narrative: THIS PROGRAM PROJECT PROVIDES ASSISTANCE AGREEMENTS TO STATES, TERRITORIES AND TRIBES TO IMPLEMENT THE ENERGY POLICY ACT OF 2005 (EPACT).  FOR STATES AND TERRITORIES, THE OBJECTIVE OF THIS FUNDING ASSISTANCE IS TO ASSIST WITH INSPECTIONS AND OTHER RELEASE PREVENTION AND COMPLIANCE ASSURANCE ACTIVITIES FOR FEDERALLY-REGULATED UNDERGROUND STORAGE TANKS (USTS), AS WELL AS FOR ENFORCEMENT ACTIVITIES RELATED TO RELEASE PREVENTION. AS A GENERAL RULE, ELIGIBLE ACTIVITIES ARE THOSE ACTIVITIES THAT ARE REASONABLY NECESSARY TO CARRY OUT THE ACTIVITIES AUTHORIZED IN SECTIONS 9003(I), 9003(J), 9005(C), 9010, 9011, AND 9012 OF THE SOLID WASTE DISPOSAL ACT.  PRIORITY WILL BE GIVEN TO PROVIDING FUNDS TO ENABLE THE STATES TO MEET THEIR RESPONSIBILITIES UNDER TITLE XV, SUBTITLE B OF THE ENERGY POLICY ACT OF 2005.  STATES THAT HAVE ENTERED INTO COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS WITH EPA MUST HAVE THE AUTHORITY TO INSPECT AND TAKE OTHER COMPLIANCE AND RELATED ENFORCEMENT ACTIONS TO PREVENT RELEASES FROM USTS.  EPA MAY ALSO PROVIDE IN KIND CONTRACTUAL ASSISTANCE TO RECIPIENTS OF LUST PREVENTION COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS WITH LUST TRUST FUND RESOURCES. EPA HAS THE PRIMARY RESPONSIBILITY FOR IMPLEMENTATION OF THE UST PROGRAM IN INDIAN COUNTRY.  FOR TRIBES, THE LUST PREVENTION ASSISTANCE AGREEMENTS ARE FOR ANY ACTIONS NECESSARY TO IMPLEMENT THE UST PREVENTION PROGRAM, AS HIGHLIGHTED IN EPA?S ?STRATEGY FOR AN EPA/TRIBAL PARTNERSHIP TO IMPLEMENT SECTION 1529 OF THE ENERGY POLICY ACT OF 2005.?  GRANTS UNDER P.L. 105-276 AUTHORITY WILL CONTINUE TO HELP TRIBES DEVELOP THE CAPACITY TO ADMINISTER LUST PROGRAMS.  FOR EXAMPLE, FUNDING IS USED TO SUPPORT TRAINING FOR TRIBAL STAFF, EDUCATE OWNERS AND OPERATORS IN INDIAN COUNTRY ABOUT UST REQUIREMENTS, AND MAINTAIN INFORMATION ON USTS LOCATED IN INDIAN COUNTRY. NOTE:  PRIOR TO FY 2008, BEFORE THE LUST PREVENTION APPROPRIATION, STAG FUNDS COULD BE USED IN STAND-ALONE CATEGORICAL UST STATE GRANTS AND PERFORMANCE PARTNERSHIP GRANTS (PPGS)/PERFORMANCE PARTNERSHIP AGREEMENTS (PPAS).  IN FY 2008, CONGRESS APPROPRIATED THE LUST PREVENTION FUNDS IN THE LUST APPROPRIATION.  WHILE LUST PREVENTION FUNDS CAN BE COMBINED WITH  CATEGORICAL UST STATE GRANTS BECAUSE THE ACTIVITY IS FOR THE PREVENTION OF RELEASES FROM UNDERGROUND STORAGE TANKS, LUST PREVENTION FUNDS CANNOT BE COMBINED WITH STAG PPGS/PPAS BECAUSE THE LUST APPROPRIATION FUNDS CANNOT BE PUT INTO PPGS/PPAS.  REGIONS MUST REFER TO THE LUST PREVENTION PROGRAM GUIDANCE OF MAY 8, 2008 IN AWARDING LUST PREVENTION ASSISTANCE AGREEMENTS.
Definition: LUST PREVENTION PROGRAM FUNDS ARE USED TO PROVIDE RESOURCES TO STATES, TERRITORIES, TRIBES AND INTERTRIBAL CONSORTIA FOR THEIR UNDERGROUND STORAGE TANK (UST) PROGRAMS. MAJOR ACTIVITIES FOCUS ON INSPECTIONS, ENFORCEMENT, DEVELOPMENT OF LEAK PREVENTION REGULATIONS AND OTHER PROGRAM INFRASTRUCTURE.  TRIBAL UST ASSISTANCE AGREEMENTS FUNDED WITH LUST PREVENTION RESOURCES INCLUDE ALL ASPECTS OF THE TRIBAL PROGRAMS, E.G., INSPECTION CAPACITY.  EPA SHALL NOT REPROGRAM THESE FUNDS OUT OF THIS PROGRAM PROJECT; NOR ARE THESE FUNDS TO BE USED FOR ANY PURPOSE OTHER THAN WHAT IS DEFINED HERE.

Name: GEOGRAPHIC PROGRAM: GREAT LAKES RESTORATION INITIATIVE
Title: J7 - GEOGRAPHIC PROGRAM: GREAT LAKES RESTORATION INITIATIVE
Laws: CLEAN WATER ACT SECTION 118 CLEAN WATER ACT SECTION 104(B) ADMINISTRATIVE PROVISIONS OF FY 2010 APPROPRIATION FOR EPA
Narrative: EPA'S GREAT LAKES (GL) PROGRAM COORDINATES ACTIVITIES AND IMPLEMENTS PROGRAMS AND PROJECTS TO RESTORE AND MAINTAIN THE CHEMICAL, PHYSICAL, AND BIOLOGICAL INTEGRITY OF THE GL BASIN ECOSYSTEM AS REQUIRED BY THE GREAT LAKES WATER QUALITY AGREEMENT AND THE CLEAN WATER ACT. GL RESTORATION INITIATIVE FUNDING THROUGH GRANTS, COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS, CONTRACTS, AND INTERAGENCY AGREEMENTS IS PROVIDED THROUGH EPA'S GL NATIONAL PROGRAM OFFICE FOR THE FULL RANGE OF GL PROTECTION AND RESTORATION ACTIVITIES, OTHER THAN TRADITIONAL INFRASTRUCTURE FUNDING THAT IS ADDRESSED UNDER THE CLEAN WATER OR DRINKING WATER STATE REVOLVING FUND PROGRAM. THE AGENCY IS WORKING TO REDUCE ITS CARBON FOOTPRINT BY PROMOTING GREEN TRAVEL PRACTICES AND MOVING ROUTINE MEETINGS TO A WEB OR VIDEO CONFERENCE FORMAT. IN ORDER TO BE SUCCESSFUL, STRATEGIC INVESTMENTS IN VIDEO/WEB CONFERENCING CAPABILITIES ARE NECESSARY TO CREATE MULTI-USE CONFERENCE ROOMS IN SELECTED LOCATIONS, AS WELL AS THE NEEDED INTERNET CAPACITY.ACTIVITIES ARE GUIDED BY A DRAFT GL RESTORATION INITIATIVE ACTION PLAN, BUILT UPON THE FOUNDATION OF THE 2005 GL REGIONAL COLLABORATION STRATEGY, AND ARE DIRECTED AMONGST FIVE FOCUS AREAS:TOXIC SUBSTANCES AND AREAS OF CONCERN. THIS AREA INCLUDES, BUT IS NOT LIMITED TO: RESTORATION OF AREAS OF CONCERN AND BENEFICIAL USE IMPAIRMENTS; REMEDIATION OF CONTAMINATED SEDIMENTS, INCLUDING WORK UNDER THE GL LEGACY ACT; STRATEGIC POLLUTION PREVENTION AND REDUCTION PROJECTS; PROTECTION OF HUMAN HEALTH THROUGH SAFER FISH CONSUMPTION; AND MEASURING PROGRESS AND ASSESSING NEW TOXIC THREATS. INVASIVE SPECIES. THIS AREA INCLUDES, BUT IS NOT LIMITED TO: ADVANCING THE DEVELOPMENT OF BALLAST WATER TREATMENT THAT PROTECTS FRESHWATER ECOSYSTEMS; ANALYSIS AND IMPLEMENTATION OF ACTIONS TO ADDRESS MOVEMENT THROUGH CANALS AND WATERWAYS; PROMOTING PREVENTION ACTIVITIES BY BROAD STAKEHOLDER OUTREACH AND EDUCATION; DEVELOPMENT AND DEMONSTRATION OF INNOVATIVE CONTROL TECHNOLOGY; SUPPORT FOR STATE INVASIVE SPECIES PREVENTION AND CONTROL ACTIONS; CONTROL OF KEY INVASIVE SPECIES; INVESTIGATION OF CAUSAL MECHANISMS BY WHICH INVASIVE SPECIES CAN IMPACT NATIVE SPECIES; AND ESTABLISHMENT OF EARLY DETECTION AND RAPID RESPONSE CAPABILITY. NEARSHORE HEALTH AND NONPOINT SOURCE POLLUTION. THIS AREA INCLUDES, BUT IS NOT LIMITED TO: WATERSHED PLAN IMPLEMENTATION; IDENTIFICATION OF SOURCES AND REDUCTION OF LOADINGS OF NUTRIENTS AND SOIL EROSION; IMPROVEMENT OF PUBLIC HEALTH PROTECTION AT BEACHES; AND GENERATION OF CRITICAL INFORMATION AND ACTIONS FOR PROTECTING NEARSHORE HEALTH. HABITAT AND WILDLIFE PROTECTION AND RESTORATION. THIS AREA INCLUDES, BUT IS NOT LIMITED TO: IMPROVING AQUATIC ECOSYSTEM RESILIENCY, MAINTAINING OR IMPROVING THE POPULATION STATUS OF THREATENED, ENDANGERED, RARE, AND MIGRATORY SPECIES; ENHANCING WETLANDS, WETLAND-ASSOCIATED UPLANDS, AND HIGH PRIORITY COASTAL, UPLAND, AND ISLAND HABITATS; IDENTIFICATION, INVENTORY, AND TRACKING PROGRESS ON GL HABITATS, INCLUDING COASTAL WETLAND RESTORATION; AND RESTORATION OF HABIT FUNCTIONING IN AREAS OF CONCERN. ACCOUNTABILITY, EDUCATION, MONITORING, EVALUATION, COMMUNICATION, AND PARTNERSHIPS. THIS AREA INCLUDES, BUT IS NOT LIMITED TO: DEVELOPMENT OF AN ACCOUNTABILITY SYSTEM; MEASURING AND EVALUATING THE HEALTH OF THE GL USING THE BEST AVAILABLE SCIENCE; SUPPORT FOR GL RESTORATION EDUCATION; ENHANCING PARTNERSHIPS, INCLUDING COORDINATION WITH THE EIGHT GL STATES, CANADA, AND THE INTERNATIONAL JOINT COMMISSION; BASIN-WIDE POLICY DEVELOPMENT AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE; OVERALL IMPLEMENTATION AND COORDINATION FOR LAKEWIDE MANAGEMENT PLANS; AND DEVELOPMENT OF REPORTS TO CONGRESS AND THE IJ
Definition: THE GOAL OF THE GREAT LAKES (GL) PROGRAM IS TO RESTORE AND MAINTAIN THE CHEMICAL, PHYSICAL, AND BIOLOGICAL INTEGRITY OF THE GREAT LAKES BASIN ECOSYSTEM AS REQUIRED BY THE GREAT LAKES WATER QUALITY AGREEMENT AND THE CLEAN WATER ACT. EPA LEADS 16 FEDERAL AGENCIES IN DEVELOPMENT AND IMPLEMENTATION OF A GL RESTORATION INITIATIVE TO ACHIEVE THAT GOAL, INCLUDING OVERSEEING INTERNATIONAL COMMITMENTS IN THE GL WATER QUALITY AGREEMENT. GL RESTORATION INITIATIVE FUNDING IS PROVIDED THROUGH EPA'S GL NATIONAL PROGRAM OFFICE, AS DESCRIBED BELOW.

Name: GEOGRAPHIC PROGRAM:  SOUTH FLORIDA
Title: K2 - GEOGRAPHIC PROGRAM:  SOUTH FLORIDA
Laws: FLORIDA KEYS NATIONAL MARINE SANCTUARY AND PROTECTION ACT OF 1990;NATIONAL MARINE SANCTUARIES PROGRAM AMENDMENTS ACT OF 1992; CLEAN WATER ACT (CWA); WATER RESOURCES DEVELOPMENT ACT OF 1996; ANDWATER RESOURCES DEVELOPMENT ACT OF 2000.
Narrative: EPA IMPLEMENTS, COORDINATES, AND FACILITATES ACTIVITIES INCLUDING THE CLEAN WATER ACT (CWA) SECTION 404 WETLANDS PROTECTION PROGRAM, THE COMPREHENSIVE EVERGLADES RESTORATION PROGRAM (CERP), THE WATER QUALITY PROTECTION PROGRAM FOR THE FLORIDA KEYS NATIONAL MARINE SANCTUARY (FKNMS), THE FKNMS WATER QUALITY MONITORING PROGRAM, THE CORAL REEF ENVIRONMENTAL MONITORING PROGRAM, THE BENTHIC HABITAT MONITORING PROGRAM, THE SOUTHEAST FLORIDA CORAL REEF INITIATIVE (SEFCRI) AS DIRECTED BY THE U.S. CORAL REEF TASK FORCE, THE BROWNFIELDS PROGRAM, AND OTHER PROGRAMS
Definition: THE SOUTH FLORIDA PROGRAM LEADS SPECIAL INITIATIVES AND PLANNING ACTIVITIES IN THE SOUTH FLORIDA REGION, WHICH INCLUDES THE EVERGLADES AND FLORIDA KEYS CORAL REEF ECOSYSTEM.

Name: GEOGRAPHIC PROGRAM:  MISSISSIPPI RIVER BASIN
Title: K3 - GEOGRAPHIC PROGRAM:  MISSISSIPPI RIVER BASIN
Laws: CLEAN WATER ACT
Narrative: RESOURCES IN THIS PROGRAM PROJECT ? SUPPLEMENTED BY EPA SUPPORT VIA THE SURFACE WATER PROTECTION AND GULF OF MEXICO PROGRAMS ? SUPPORT GRANTS FOR THE IMPLEMENTATION OF STATE NUTRIENT REDUCTION STRATEGIES, CONSISTENT WITH  ACTIONS OUTLINED IN THE HYPOXIA ACTION PLAN 2008, AND THE ACTION PLAN II.  THE 2008 ACTION PLAN DESCRIBES THREE GOALS AND ELEVEN ACTIONS NEEDED TO REDUCE NITROGEN AND PHOSPHORUS, INCLUDING THE PROMOTION OF EFFECTIVE CONSERVATION PRACTICES AND MANAGEMENT PRACTICES, TRACKING PROGRESS, REDUCING EXISTING SCIENTIFIC UNCERTAINTIES, IDENTIFYING THE ECONOMIC COSTS OF HYPOXIA, AND PROMOTING EFFECTIVE COMMUNICATIONS TO INCREASE AWARENESS OF GULF HYPOXIA. EPA?S WORK WILL CONTINUE TO INVOLVE CLOSE COLLABORATION WITH THE U. S. DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE?S (USDA) EFFORTS TO TARGET CRITICAL WATERSHEDS FOR FOCUSED NUTRIENT REDUCTION EFFORTS AND THE EFFORTS OF THE U.S. GEOLOGICAL SURVEY (USGS) TO MEASURE PROGRESS IN NUTRIENT REDUCTION WITHIN THE BASIN.  EPA WILL FOCUS ON THE MOST SIGNIFICANT CONTRIBUTORS TO SEDIMENT, NITROGEN, AND PHOSPHORUS LOADINGS AT A STATE AND WATERSHED SCALE IN SELECTING WHERE TO AWARD FUNDS.
Definition: TARGETED EFFORTS TO THE HIGHEST PRIORITY WATERSHEDS IN THE MISSISSIPPI RIVER BASIN TO DEMONSTRATE HOW EFFECTIVE NUTRIENT REDUCTION STRATEGIES AND ENHANCED PARTNERSHIPS CAN YIELD SIGNIFICANT PROGRESS IN ADDRESSING NON-POINT SOURCE DRIVEN NUTRIENT POLLUTION.

Name: GEOGRAPHIC PROGRAM:  SAN FRANCISCO BAY
Title: K4 - GEOGRAPHIC PROGRAM:  SAN FRANCISCO BAY
Laws: CLEAN WATER ACT (CWA)
Narrative: PARTICIPATION IN FEDERAL AND STATE PARTNERSHIPS AIMED AT RESOLVING THE CHALLENGES OF WATER QUALITY, ECOSYSTEM HEALTH AND WATER SUPPLIES;WATER QUALITY IMPROVEMENTS THROUGH TOTAL MAXIMUM DAILY LOADS (TMDLS), NATIONAL POLLUTANT DISCHARGE ELIMINATION SYSTEM (NPDES), NONPOINT SOURCE PROGRAM, WATERSHED PLANS AND UPGRADING AGING INFRASTRUCTURE;SUPPORT THE SAN FRANCISCO ESTUARY PARTNERSHIP (NATIONAL ESTUARY PROGRAM) AND THE IMPLEMENTATION OF THE COMPREHENSIVE CONSERVATION AND MANAGEMENT PLAN (CCMP);PROTECT AND RESTORE STREAMS AND WETLANDS, AND THE REUSE OF DREDGE MATERIAL; ANDPREDICTING, MITIGATING AND ADAPTING TO CLIMATE CHANGE IMPACTS ON WATER QUALITY.
Definition: THE SAN FRANCISCO BAY-DELTA ESTUARY PROGRAM IS AIMED AT PROTECTING AND RESTORING WATER QUALITY AND ECOLOGICAL HEALTH OF THE ESTUARY THROUGH PARTNERSHIPS, INTERAGENCY COORDINATION, AND PROJECT GRANTS.

Name: GEOGRAPHIC PROGRAM:  PUGET SOUND
Title: K5 - GEOGRAPHIC PROGRAM:  PUGET SOUND
Laws: CLEAN WATER ACT (CWA); WATER RESOURCES DEVELOPMENT ACT OF 1996; WATER RESOURCES DEVELOPMENT ACT OF 2000; RESOURCE CONSERVATION AND RECOVERY ACT OF 1976 (RCRA); COMPREHENSIVE ENVIRONMENTAL RESPONSE COMPENSATION AND LIABILITY ACT (CERCLA); ECONOMY ACT OF 1932; INTERGOVERNMENTAL COOPERATION ACT; CLEAN AIR ACT (CAA); SAFE DRINKING WATER ACT (SWDA); TOXIC SUBSTANCES CONTROL ACT (TSCA); FEDERAL INSECTICIDE, FUNGICIDE AND RODENTICIDE ACT (FIFRA); POLLUTION PREVENTION ACT; MARINE PROTECTION, RESEARCH, AND SANCTUARIES ACT; NATIONAL ENVIRONMENTAL EDUCATION ACT
Narrative: IMPROVING WATER QUALITY AND UPGRADING SHELLFISH BED CLASSIFICATIONS; MANAGING STORMWATER BY IMPLEMENTING EFFECTIVE LOCAL WATERSHED PROTECTION       PLANS;REDUCING SOURCES OF TOXICS AND NUTRIENTS;RESTORING AND PROTECTING NEAR SHORE HABITAT; ANDIMPROVING MONITORING AND SCIENCE.
Definition: THE PUGET SOUND PROGRAM WORKS TO PROTECT AND RESTORE THE PUGET SOUND WHICH HAS BEEN DESIGNATED AS AN ESTUARY OF NATIONAL SIGNIFICANCE UNDER THE CLEAN WATER ACT NATIONAL ESTUARY PROGRAM (NEP).  EPA EFFORTS ARE FOCUSED ON HIGH PRIORITY ENVIRONMENTAL ACTIVITIES CONSISTENT WITH THE STATE OF WASHINGTON?S 2020 PUGET SOUND ACTION AGENDA.

Name: RESEARCH:  AIR, CLIMATE AND ENERGY
Title: K6 - RESEARCH:  AIR, CLIMATE AND ENERGY
Laws: CAA 42 U.S.C. 7401 ET SEQ. TITLE 1, PART A ? SEC. 103 (A) AND (D) AND SEC. 104 (C); CAA 42U.S.C 7402(B) SECTION 102; CAA 42 U.S.C 7403(B)(2) SECTION 103(B)(2); CLINGER COHEN ACT, 40U.S.C 11318; ECONOMY ACT, 31 U.S.C 1535; EISA, TITLE II SUBTITLE B; ERDDA, 33 U.S.C. 1251? SECTION 2(A); INTERGOVERNMENTAL COOPERATION ACT, 31 U.S.C. 6502; NCPA; NEPA, SECTION 102;PPA; USGCRA 15 U.S.C. 2921.
Narrative: ACE SUPPORTS INTEGRATED AND TARGETED RESEARCH TO: ASSESS IMPACTS ? ASSESS HUMAN AND ECOSYSTEM EXPOSURES AND EFFECTS ASSOCIATED WITH AIR POLLUTANTS AND CLIMATE CHANGE AT INDIVIDUAL, COMMUNITY, REGIONAL, AND GLOBAL SCALES.PREVENT AND REDUCE EMISSIONS ? PROVIDE DATA AND TOOLS TO DEVELOP AND EVALUATE APPROACHES TO PREVENT AND REDUCE EMISSIONS OF POLLUTANTS TO THE ATMOSPHERE, PARTICULARLY ENVIRONMENTALLY SUSTAINABLE, COST-EFFECTIVE, AND INNOVATIVE MULTIPOLLUTANT AND SECTOR-BASED APPROACHES. RESPOND TO CHANGES IN CLIMATE AND AIR QUALITY ? PROVIDE HUMAN EXPOSURE AND ENVIRONMENTAL MODELING, MONITORING, METRICS AND INFORMATION NEEDED BY INDIVIDUALS, COMMUNITIES, AND GOVERNMENTAL AGENCIES TO ADAPT TO THE IMPACTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND MAKE PUBLIC HEALTH DECISIONS REGARDING AIR QUALITY. ADVANCE AIR POLLUTION SCIENCES TO REDUCE UNCERTAINTY IN AIR QUALITY STANDARD SETTING AND MANAGEMENT DECISIONS IN ACCORDANCE WITH EPA?S LEGISLATIVE MANDATES FOR PERIODIC REVISION OF THE NATIONAL AMBIENT AIR QUALITY STANDARDS (NAAQS) AND HAZARDOUS AIR POLLUTANT (HAP) ASSESSMENTS.REDUCE UNCERTAINTIES IN LINKING HEALTH AND ENVIRONMENTAL OUTCOMES TO AIR POLLUTION SOURCES TO SUPPORT EFFECTIVE AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES.ADVANCE AIR POLLUTION SCIENCE BY INCORPORATING A MORE INNOVATIVE MULTI-POLLUTANT RESEARCH APPROACH.PERFORM RESEARCH, IN COLLABORATION WITH OTHER PROGRAMS TO INFORM EFFORTS TO MITIGATE GREENHOUSE GASES AND OTHER RADIOACTIVE FORCING COMPOUNDS. AIR QUALITY: MODEL AND INTEGRATE THE COMBINED EFFECTS OF GLOBAL ATMOSPHERIC PROCESS (E.G., LONG-RANGE TRANSPORT OF POLLUTANTS; CHANGES IN THE STRATOSPHERIC OZONE LAYER) AND REGIONAL AND LOCAL PROCESSES (E.G., LOCAL EMISSIONS OF POLLUTANTS FROM TRANSPORTATION; CHANGES IN LAND-USE PATTERNS).MODEL PHYSICAL AND BIOLOGICAL PROCESSES (E.G., SALT WATER INTRUSION DUE TO SEA LEVEL RISE; MORE RUNOFF DUE TO INCREASED LEVELS AND INTENSITY OF PRECIPITATION), COMBINED WITH HUMAN DEMANDS (E.G., DUE TO CHANGES IN LAND-USE PATTERNS) AND INSTITUTIONAL PROCESSES, TO FACILITATE EFFICIENT WATER RESOURCES MANAGEMENT.UNDERSTAND AND MODEL THE VULNERABILITY OF AQUATIC ECOSYSTEMS TO GLOBAL CHANGE, IDENTIFYING OPTIONS FOR ENHANCING RESILIENCE AND SUSTAINING ECOSYSTEM GOODS AND SERVICES.UNDERSTAND AND MODEL THE INTERACTIONS BETWEEN AIR QUALITY, WATER QUALITY, ECOSYSTEMS, AND HUMAN HEALTH (E.G., IMPLICATIONS OF CHANGES IN WATER QUALITY FOR THE SUSTAINABILITY OF ECOSYSTEM SERVICES).MODEL THE HUMAN CONSEQUENCES OF GLOBAL CHANGE, PARTICULARLY FOR THE KEY LIFE-SUPPORT SYSTEMS OF AIR, WATER, AND ECOSYSTEMS.PROVIDE SUPPORT TO DECISION MAKERS WITH AREAS OF RESPONSIBILITY LIKELY TO BE AFFECTED BY CLIMATE CHANGE, SUCH AS AIR QUALITY DISTRICT MANAGERS, STATE ENVIRONMENTAL AGENCIES, WATERSHED MANAGERS, AND OPERATORS OF WASTE AND DRINKING WATER SYSTEMS.DEVELOP AND APPLY TOOLS AND METHODOLOGIES FOR IMPROVING INDIVIDUAL AND ORGANIZATIONAL DECISION MAKING TO REDUCE OR ELIMINATE EMISSIONS, EFFLUENTS, AND WASTES FROM PRODUCTS AND/OR PROCESSES THROUGH THE DEVELOPMENT AND USE OF SUSTAINABLE MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES.CONTINUE THE BIOFUELS RESEARCH INITIATIVE STARTED IN FY 2010 TO HELP DECISION MAKERS BETTER UNDERSTAND THE RISK TRADEOFFS ASSOCIATED WITH BIOFUELS USE AND PRODUCTION.
Definition: THE AIR, CLIMATE AND ENERGY (ACE) RESEARCH PROGRAM PROMOTES INNOVATIVE, SUSTAINABLE, AND INTEGRATED SOLUTIONS TO AIR POLLUTION AND CLIMATE CHANGE.  FOR EXAMPLE, THE ACE PROGRAM PROVIDES RESEARCH TO EPA?S PROGRAMS TO IMPROVE PUBLIC HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT, INCREASE LIFE EXPECTANCY, AND PROTECT THE MOST SUSCEPTIBLE POPULATIONS. ACE PROVIDES MODELS AND TOOLS NECESSARY FOR POLICY MAKERS AT ALL LEVELS OF COMMUNITY AND GOVERNMENT TO MAKE THE BEST DECISIONS. ACE?S INTEGRATED RESEARCH SUPPORTS THE AGENCY?S IMPLEMENTATION OF THE CLEAN AIR ACT (CAA), ESPECIALLY THE NATIONAL AMBIENT AIR QUALITY STANDARDS (NAAQS), WHICH SET LIMITS ON HOW MUCH TROPOSPHERIC OZONE, PARTICULATE MATTER, CARBON MONOXIDE, SULFUR DIOXIDE, NITROGEN OXIDES, AND LEAD ARE ALLOWED IN THE ATMOSPHERE.  THE PROGRAM ALSO EVALUATES THE RISKS ASSOCIATED WITH HAZARDOUS AIR POLLUTANTS, ALSO KNOWN AS AIR TOXICS.  ACE IS AN ACTIVE PARTICIPANT IN THE U.S. GLOBAL CHANGE RESEARCH PROGRAM (USGCRP), THE INTERAGENCY FEDERAL EFFORT TO IMPROVE SCIENTIFIC UNDERSTANDING OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND GLOBAL CHANGE. BEGINNING IN FY2012, THE FOLLOWING RESEARCH PROGRAMS WILL BE INTEGRATED INTO THE AIR, CLIMATE AND ENERGY RESEARCH PROGRAM:-CLEAN AIR RESEARCH-GLOBAL CHANGE RESEARCH-BIOFUELS RESEARCH (WITHIN THE SUSTAINABILITY PROGRAM)

Name: RESEARCH:  SAFE AND SUSTAINABLE WATER RESOURCES
Title: K7 - RESEARCH:  SAFE AND SUSTAINABLE WATER RESOURCES
Laws: SDWA PART E, SEC. 1442 (A)(1); CWA TITLE I, SEC. 101(A)(6) 33 U.S.C. 1254 ? SEC 104 (A) AND (C) AND SEC. 105; ERDDA 33 U.S.C. 1251 ? SECTION 2(A); MPRSA SEC. 203, 33 U.S.C. 1443; ODBA TITLE II; SPA; CVA; WRDA; WWWQA; MPPRCA; NISA; CZARA;, CWPPRA; ESA; NAWCA; FIFRA 7 U.S. C. 135 ET SEQ; TSCA U.S. C. 136 ET SEQ.
Narrative: SSWR RESEARCH WILL GUIDE THE NATIONAL IMPLEMENTATION OF EPA?S REGULATORY AND NON-REGULATORY EFFORTS BY CONDUCTING/SUPPORTING RESEARCH AND DEVELOPMENT TO: PROTECT AND RESTORE WATERSHEDS TO PROVIDE SAFE AND SUSTAINABLE WATER QUALITY NECESSARY FOR HUMAN AND ECOSYSTEM HEALTH; DETERMINE A SUSTAINABLE APPROACH TO WATER QUALITY AND AVAILABILITY TO SUPPORT THE NEEDS OF HEALTHY HUMANS, ECOSYSTEMS, AND ECONOMIES; AND DEVELOP A WATER INFRASTRUCTURE CAPABLE OF THE SUSTAINED DELIVERY OF SAFE WATER, PROVIDING FOR THE REMOVAL AND TREATMENT OF WASTEWATER CONSISTENT WITH ITS SUSTAINABLE AND SAFE REUSE, AND MANAGEMENT OF STORMWATER IN A MANNER THAT VALUES IT AS A RESOURCE AND A COMPONENT OF SUSTAINABLE WATER RESOURCES. DETERMINE THE CRITICAL SCIENCE QUESTIONS IMPACTING THE DEVELOPMENT AND MAINTENANCE OF SAFE, SUSTAINABLE WATERS. UNDERSTAND THE KEY WATER ISSUES SUCH AS COMPREHENSIVE WATER RESOURCE MANAGEMENT, WATER SUSTAINABILITY METRICS, INFRASTRUCTURE LIFE-CYCLE ASSESSMENTS, AND ECONOMICAL AND EFFECTIVE MANAGEMENT OF STRESSORS (E.G., NUTRIENTS, SEDIMENTS, PATHOGENS OTHER CONTAMINANTS).DETERMINE WHETHER HYDRAULIC FRACTURING HAS ADVERSE EFFECTS ON DRINKING WATER RESOURCES IN RESPONSE PROVIDE NEW TOOLS FOR INFRASTRUCTURE CONDITION ASSESSMENT, REPAIR, REPLACEMENT, AND REHABILITATION. SUPPORT SEVERAL REGIONAL GREEN INFRASTRUCTURE PROJECTS. THIS WORK WILL PROVIDE BETTER PREDICTIVE TOOLS AND GUIDANCE FOR SELECTING AND IMPLEMENTING APPROPRIATE GREEN TECHNOLOGIES. INVESTIGATE POTENTIAL IMPACTS OF A CHANGING CLIMATE ON WATER RESOURCES. DETERMINE EXISTING INFRASTRUCTURE PROBLEMS ASSOCIATED WITH BUILT URBAN ENVIRONMENTS AND SPRAWL,UNDERSTAND POTENTIAL CONSEQUENCES OF INCREASED ENERGY DEMAND AND MINERAL EXTRACTION ON WATER QUALITY.SUPPORT MAINTENANCE AND USE OF NATURAL AND ENGINEERED AQUATIC SYSTEMS TO FULLY ENSURE THE NEEDED CAPACITY AND QUALITY OF WATER THAT SUPPORTS THE NATION?S RANGE OF GROWING DEMANDS AND USES.
Definition: IN FY 2013, THE SAFE AND SUSTAINABLE WATER RESOURCES RESEARCH (SSWR) PROGRAM TAKES A SYSTEMS APPROACH TO PROTECTING HUMAN AND AQUATIC ECOSYSTEM HEALTH AND PROTECTING AND RESTORING WATERSHEDS FOR THE SUSTAINABILITY OF THE NATION?S WATER RESOURCES. THE PROGRAM SEEKS SAFE, RESILIENT AND SUSTAINABLE SOLUTIONS TO THE INCREASINGLY COMPLEX WATER CHALLENGES FACING THE NATION?S REGIONS, STATES, TRIBES, CITIES, AND RURAL AREAS.  THE PROGRAM IS WORKING TO ADDRESS THE INCREASING COMPLEX CHALLENGES WITH SOLUTIONS THAT ARE EFFECTIVE, EFFICIENT, AND SUSTAINABLE ? SOLUTIONS THAT ARE DESIGNED TO MEET CURRENT NEEDS WHILE MINIMIZING POTENTIAL HEALTH AND ENVIRONMENTAL DETRIMENT IN THE FUTURE. THE FOLLOWING ARE DESCRIPTIONS OF FY 2013 SSWR ACTIVITIES AS CATEGORIZED UNDER EACH PROGRAM AREA:ï¿½EPA?S DRINKING WATER RESEARCH PROGRAM CONDUCTS COMPREHENSIVE INTEGRATED RESEARCH IN SUPPORT OF EPA?S WATER PROGRAM AND REGIONAL OFFICES? IMPLEMENTATION OF THE SAFE DRINKING WATER ACT (SDWA). THE PROGRAM FOCUSES ON CHARACTERIZATION AND MANAGEMENT OF HUMAN HEALTH RISKS ACROSS THE WATER CONTINUUM WITH AN EMPHASIS ON SOUND SCIENTIFIC APPROACHES FOR ENSURING SAFE AND SUSTAINABLE DRINKING WATER.ï¿½THE WATER QUALITY RESEARCH PROGRAM IS DESIGNED TO SUPPORT THE CLEAN WATER ACT (CWA), PROVIDING SCIENTIFIC INFORMATION AND TOOLS TO THE AGENCY AND OTHERS TO HELP PROTECT AND RESTORE THE DESIGNATED USES OF WATER BODIES THAT SUSTAIN HUMAN HEALTH AND AQUATIC LIFE. RESEARCH FOCUSES ON THE DEVELOPMENT AND APPLICATION OF WATER QUALITY CRITERIA, THE IMPLEMENTATION OF EFFECTIVE WATERSHED MANAGEMENT APPROACHES, AND THE APPLICATION OF TECHNOLOGICAL OPTIONS TO RESTORE AND PROTECT WATER BODIES USING INFORMATION ON EFFECTIVE IDENTIFICATION, TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT ALTERNATIVES.
ProgramAreaName: RESEARCH:  SAFE AND SUSTAINABLE WATER RESOURCES

Name: RESEARCH:  SAFER PRODUCTS FOR A SUSTAINABLE WORLD
Title: K9 - RESEARCH:  SAFER PRODUCTS FOR A SUSTAINABLE WORLD
Laws: CAA, SEC. 103, 104 & 154; CCA, 40 U.S.C 11318; CERCLA; CHILDREN?S HEALTH ACT; 21ST CENTURY NANOTECHNOLOGY RESEARCH AND DEVELOPMENT ACT, 15 U.S.C. 750; CWA, SEC. 101 - 121; ECONOMY ACT, 31 U.S.C 1535; ERDDAA, 42 U.S.C. 4361-4370; FFDCA, 21 U.S.C. SEC. 346; FIFRA; FQPA; INTERGOVERNMENTAL COOPERATION ACT, 31 U.S.C. 6502; NATIONAL ENVIRONMENTAL POLICY ACT OF 1969, SECTION 102; PPA, 42 U.S.C. 13103; RCRA; SDWA, 42 U.S.C.; TSCA, SECTION 10, 15, 26 U.S.C.
Narrative: USE HIGH-THROUGHPUT ASSAYS TO IDENTIFY KEY LINKAGES BETWEEN THE PRODUCTION OF A CHEMICAL, ITS RELEASE INTO THE ENVIRONMENT, THE RESULTING EXPOSURES, AND ADVERSE OUTCOMES FOR PEOPLE AND THE NATURAL ENVIRONMENT. DEVELOP BIOMARKERS OF EXPOSURE THAT ENABLE THE CHARACTERIZATION OF ADVERSE OUTCOMES PATHWAYS IN EXPOSED POPULATIONS. USE ADVANCED COMPUTATIONAL TECHNIQUES SUCH AS MULTI-SCALE SYSTEMS MODELS OF VIRTUAL TISSUES TO IMPROVE QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT AND IMPROVE CONFIDENCE IN EXTRAPOLATION MODELS. GENERATE CHEMICAL EVALUATION STRATEGIES THAT INTEGRATE DECISION MAKERS? NEEDS, THUS IMPROVING THE SCIENTIFIC BASIS FOR THEIR RISK ASSESSMENTS AND RISK MANAGEMENT DECISIONS.IMPROVE EXISTING METHODS TO UNDERSTAND INHERENT PROPERTIES AND PREDICT BEHAVIORS AND IMPACTS OF CHEMICALS AND THEIR RELATED PRODUCTS THROUGHOUT THEIR LIFE-CYCLE. DEVELOP THE NEXT GENERATION OF RISK ASSESSMENT METHODS THAT ARE MORE RESPONSIVE TO THE VARIED SPECIFIC DECISION CONTEXTS; DEVELOP SCIENTIFIC KNOWLEDGE, TOOLS, AND MODELS NEEDED TO OPTIMIZE DATA NEEDS FOR DIFFERENT TYPES OF ASSESSMENT AND MANAGEMENT DECISIONS. COMBINE HIGH-THROUGHPUT SCREENING BIOASSAYS WITH COMPUTATIONAL CHEMISTRY TO BUILD STATISTICAL AND COMPUTATIONAL MODELS FOR FORECASTING POTENTIAL TOXICITY IN HUMANS AND WILDLIFE. DEFINE TOXICITY PATHWAYS BY WHICH ENDOCRINE DISRUPTORS ADVERSELY AFFECT THE HEALTH OF MAMMALIAN AND AQUATIC ORGANISMS; CHARACTERIZE THE SHAPE OF THE ?DOSE-RESPONSE? CURVE AND ITS IMPLICATIONS FOR RISK ASSESSMENT.DEVELOP APPROACHES FOR ASSESSING CUMULATIVE RISK AND METHODS FOR EXTRAPOLATING RESULTS ACROSS SPECIES, ULTIMATELY REDUCING ANIMAL TESTING.  INVESTIGATE INHERENT CHEMICAL PROPERTIES THAT INFLUENCE THE FATE, EXPOSURE AND EFFECTS OF NANOMATERIALS, WITH THE AIM OF ASCERTAINING THEIR BEHAVIORAL TRENDS AND IMPACTS. USE HIGH THROUGHPUT SCREENING AND COMPUTATIONAL MODELS TO PRIORITIZE CHEMICALS IN THE ENDOCRINE DISRUPTOR SCREENING PROGRAM (EDSP). LEVERAGE DATA FROM TOXICITY FORECASTER (TOXCAST? ? A STATE-OF-THE-ART CHEMICAL SCREENING APPROACH THAT BUILDS STATISTICAL AND COMPUTATIONAL MODELS THAT IDENTIFY AND FORECAST TOXICITY PATHWAYS RELEVANT TO HUMAN HEALTH EFFECTS) TO DEVELOP VIRTUAL FIRST GENERATION MODELS OF THE LIVER AND EMBRYO.PROVIDE DATA ON HUMAN HEALTH AND ECOLOGICAL EXPOSURE AND ASSOCIATED EFFECTS OF SELECTED PESTICIDES AND OTHER TOXIC CHEMICALS, OCCURRING SINGLY OR AS COMPLEX MIXTURES.EVALUATE LIFE CYCLE IMPACTS THAT DEMONSTRATE THE BENEFITS OF MORE SUSTAINABLE APPROACHES, INFORM MORE EFFECTIVE SOLUTIONS TO THE SUSTAINABLE MOLECULAR DESIGN OF CHEMICALS.
Definition: IN FY13, THE  CHEMICAL SAFETY AND SUSTAINABILITY PROGRAM WILL CONTINUE TO USE AN INTEGRATED RESEARCH APPROACH TO DEVELOP INNOVATIVE AND COST-EFFECTIVE APPROACHES AND TOOLS TO BETTER INFORM DECISIONS TO REDUCE HARMFUL EFFECTS OF CHEMICALS ON HUMAN HEALTH AND THE ENVIRONMENT. RESEARCH IN THE AREAS OF COMPUTATIONAL TOXICOLOGY, ENDOCRINE DISRUPTING CHEMICALS (EDCS), AND NANOTECHNOLOGY CONTINUE TO BE THE CORE COMPONENTS OF THE CSS PROGRAM.THE ULTIMATE GOAL OF THE CSS RESEARCH IS TO ENSURE SAFETY IN THE DESIGN, MANUFACTURE, AND USE OF NEW AND EXISTING CHEMICALS. THREE CONCEPTS ARE CENTRAL TO THE CSS RESEARCH PROGRAM:ï¿½LIFE CYCLE: THE CRADLE-TO-GRAVE LIFE CYCLE PERSPECTIVE TO CHEMICAL DESIGN, MANUFACTURE, USE, AND FATE, WITH THE AIM TO MANAGE POTENTIAL RISK OF EXPOSURE AND IMPACTS;ï¿½INHERENCY: THE PHYSICO-CHEMICAL AND MATERIAL PROPERTIES OF THE CHEMICAL, AND HOW THOSE PROPERTIES AFFECT THE BEHAVIOR OF CHEMICALS IN THE ENVIRONMENT; ANDï¿½SUSTAINABILITY: THE BROAD SOCIAL, ECONOMIC, AND ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACTS OF CHEMICAL USE.IN FY13, THE CSS PROGRAM WILL DIRECT ITS EFFORTS TOWARDS DEVELOPING THE SCIENTIFIC KNOWLEDGE, TOOLS, AND MODELS FOR INTEGRATED EVALUATION STRATEGIES; IMPROVE ASSESSMENT AND MANAGEMENT APPROACHES FOR CHEMICAL SAFETY AND SUSTAINABILITY, AND TARGET HIGH PRIORITY RESEARCH NEED FOR IMMEDIATE FOCUSED ACTION.   CSS RESEARCH WILL LEVERAGE THE DIVERSE CAPABILITIES OF IN-HOUSE SCIENTISTS AND ENGINEERS AND INCORPORATE INPUT FROM EXTERNAL STAKEHOLDERS SUCH AS FEDERAL, STATE AND LOCAL GOVERNMENT AGENCIES, NON-GOVERNMENTAL ORGANIZATIONS, INDUSTRY, AND COMMUNITIES AFFECTED BY ENVIRONMENTAL PROBLEMS. THIS EFFORT WILL INCREASE THE QUALITY, QUANTITY AND AVAILABILITY OF INFORMATION THAT INFORMS DECISIONS ON CHEMICAL SAFETY AS WELL AS THE INFORMATION AVAILABLE FOR DATA-POOR CHEMICALS.

Name: WATER TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE GRANT
Title: L2 - WATER TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE GRANT
Laws: SAFE DRINKING WATER ACT SECTION 1442(C), CLEAN WATER ACT 104(B)(3)
Narrative: IN 2012 CONGRESS APPROPRIATED $14.975M FOR AN ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION: NATIONAL PRIORITY COMPETITIVE GRANT PROGRAM TO PROVIDE RURAL AND URBAN COMMUNITIES WITH TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO IMPROVE WATER QUALITY AND PROVIDE SAFE DRINKING WATER.  EPA WILL AWARD FROM THREE TO SEVEN COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS TO ELIGIBLE APPLICANTS (NON-PROFIT ORGANIZATIONS, PRIVATE NON-PROFIT UNIVERSITIES AND PUBLIC INSTITUTIONS OF HIGHER EDUCATION) TOTALING $14,500,000. THE PURPOSE OF THESE COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS IS TO: 1)PROVIDE TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO SMALL PUBLIC WATER SYSTEMS TO ACHIEVE AND MAINTAIN COMPLIANCE WITH THE SAFE DRINKING WATER ACT; 2) PROVIDE TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO SMALL PUBLIC WATER SYSTEMS ON A WIDE RANGE OF MANAGERIAL AND/OR FINANCIAL SUBJECTS TO ACHIEVE AND MAINTAIN COMPLIANCE WITH THE SDWA; OR3) PROVIDE TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO SMALL PUBLICLY-OWNED WASTEWATER SYSTEMS, COMMUNITIES SERVED BY ONSITE/DECENTRALIZED WASTEWATER SYSTEMS AND PRIVATE WELL OWNERS TO HELP IMPROVE WATER QUALITY. EPA ALSO WILL AWARD ONE COOPERATIVE AGREEMENT UP TO $500,000. THE AWARDEE WILL PROVIDE TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO TRIBALLY-OWNED AND OPERATED PUBLIC WATER SYSTEMS TO HELP SUCH SYSTEMS ACHIEVE AND MAINTAIN COMPLIANCE WITH THE SAFE DRINKING WATER ACT.
Definition: COMPETITIVE GRANT TO BE AWARDED TO NON-PROFITS TO PROVIDE 1) TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO SMALL PUBLIC WATER SYSTEMS TO ACHIEVE AND MAINTAIN COMPLIANCE WITH THE SAFE DRINKING WATER ACT, AND 2) TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE FOR SMALL PUBLICLY-OWNED WASTEWATER SYSTEMS, COMMUNITIES SERVED BY ONSITE/DECENTRALIZED WASTEWATER SYSTEMS, AND PRIVATE WELL OWNER TO IMPROVE WATER QUALITY UNDER THE CLEAN WATER ACT.

Name: WATER INFRASTRUCTURE FINANCE AND INNOVATION
Title: L5 - WATER INFRASTRUCTURE FINANCE AND INNOVATION
Laws: WATER RESOURCES REFORM AND DEVELOPMENT ACT OF 2014 (WRRDA). TITLE V OF WRRDA CONTAINS WIFIA.
Narrative: WIFIA AUTHORIZES THE EPA TO PROVIDE FEDERAL CREDIT ASSISTANCE TO ELIGIBLE ENTITIES. FEDERAL CREDIT ASSISTANCE MAY BE IN THE FORM OF A DIRECT FEDERAL LOAN OR A GUARANTEE OF A LOAN OF OTHER DEBT OBLIGATION FUNDED BY AN OUTSIDE LENDER. ELIGIBLE ASSISTANCE RECIPIENTS RANGE FROM CORPORATIONS AND PARTNERSHIPS, TO MUNICIPAL ENTITIES, TO STATE REVOLVING FUND (SRF) PROGRAMS.THROUGH THIS PROGRAM EPA MANAGES AND CONDUCTS THE WIFIA PROGRAM. THE FEDERAL LOAN OR LOAN GUARANTEES WHEN FUNDED WILL BE IN A SEPARATE NO-YEAR APPROPRIATION. THE WIFIA PROGRAM WILL BE ADMINISTERED BY OW HEADQUARTERS.THIS PROGRAM (EPM APPROPRIATION) SUPPORTS WORK TO DEVELOP AND STAND-UP THE WIFIA PROGRAM AND TO MANAGE AND ADMINISTER THE PROGRAM IF NO-YEAR FUNDS ARE APPROPRIATED TO IMPLEMENT THE PROGRAM.MAJOR RESPONSIBILITIES INCLUDE:-ESTABLISH POLICIES, PROCEDURES, EVALUATION CRITERIA, APPLICATION PROCESSES, INTERNAL CONTROLS AND GOVERNANCE, AND OTHER SIMILAR EFFORTS NECESSARY TO INFORM CREDIT SUBSIDE MODELS.-DEVELOP AND MANAGE A MULTI-LOAN APPLICATION PROCESS FOR WIFIA PROJECTS, FROM CALL FOR PROJECTS TO REPAYMENT.-CONTRACT FUNDS USED FOR ADDITIONAL SPECIALIZED FINANCIAL, LEGAL, AND ENGINEERING EXPERTISE TO ADDRESS POTENTIALLY COMPLEX ISSUES.
Definition: CONDUCT THE MANAGEMENT AND ADMINISTRATIVE FUNCTIONS AS NECESSARY TO IMPLEMENT THE WATER INFRASTRUCTURE FINANCE AND INNOVATION ACT (WIFIA)

Narrative: IN 2012 CONGRESS APPROPRIATED $14.975M FOR AN ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION: NATIONAL PRIORITY COMPETITIVE GRANT PROGRAM TO PROVIDE RURAL AND URBAN COMMUNITIES WITH TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO IMPROVE WATER QUALITY AND PROVIDE SAFE DRINKING WATER.  EPA WILL AWARD FROM THREE TO SEVEN COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS TO ELIGIBLE APPLICANTS (NON-PROFIT ORGANIZATIONS, PRIVATE NON-PROFIT UNIVERSITIES AND PUBLIC INSTITUTIONS OF HIGHER EDUCATION) TOTALING $14,500,000. THE PURPOSE OF THESE COOPERATIVE AGREEMENTS IS TO: 1)PROVIDE TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO SMALL PUBLIC WATER SYSTEMS TO ACHIEVE AND MAINTAIN COMPLIANCE WITH THE SAFE DRINKING WATER ACT; 2) PROVIDE TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO SMALL PUBLIC WATER SYSTEMS ON A WIDE RANGE OF MANAGERIAL AND/OR FINANCIAL SUBJECTS TO ACHIEVE AND MAINTAIN COMPLIANCE WITH THE SDWA; OR3) PROVIDE TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO SMALL PUBLICLY-OWNED WASTEWATER SYSTEMS, COMMUNITIES SERVED BY ONSITE/DECENTRALIZED WASTEWATER SYSTEMS AND PRIVATE WELL OWNERS TO HELP IMPROVE WATER QUALITY. EPA ALSO WILL AWARD ONE COOPERATIVE AGREEMENT UP TO $500,000. THE AWARDEE WILL PROVIDE TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO TRIBALLY-OWNED AND OPERATED PUBLIC WATER SYSTEMS TO HELP SUCH SYSTEMS ACHIEVE AND MAINTAIN COMPLIANCE WITH THE SAFE DRINKING WATER ACT.
Definition: COMPETITIVE GRANT TO BE AWARDED TO NON-PROFITS TO PROVIDE 1) TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO SMALL PUBLIC WATER SYSTEMS TO ACHIEVE AND MAINTAIN COMPLIANCE WITH THE SAFE DRINKING WATER ACT, AND 2) TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE FOR SMALL PUBLICLY-OWNED WASTEWATER SYSTEMS, COMMUNITIES SERVED BY ONSITE/DECENTRALIZED WASTEWATER SYSTEMS, AND PRIVATE WELL OWNER TO IMPROVE WATER QUALITY UNDER THE CLEAN WATER ACT.
